On 26 June 2011, the imams, who were excluded for security reasons, resumed their sit-in in front of the Ministry of Endowments in Bab al Luq, demanding appointment after their sit-in in front of the Council of Ministers was broken up.

On 31 May 2011, a number of workers who were transferred to early pension in the Egyptian Tora Portland Cement Factory, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, to demand the adjustment of compensation bonus. Another group of workers returning from Algeria at Orascom Telecom, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, to demand Essam Sharaf return their financial and civil rights, treat workers equally, and provide compensation for their homes.
On 19 June 2011, a number of workers at Al Hilal Hospital in Al Fawalah, organized a protest against the treatment of the Director General of the Red Crescent Society in Giza, 'Ezzat Al-Sbaa'.

On 5 May 2011, the workers of the 'Abu Ghattas' workshops, demonstrated for the second day by standing on the rails at the Ramses station, stopping the movement of trains, demanding the establishment of an emergency ambulance unit to be used in case of emergency.

On 15 May 2011, the Students Union at Ain Shams University, with the participation of the Muslim Brotherhood and some members of the political forces, organized a protest in the university, to support the Palestinian Intifada.

On 02 April 2011, more than 200 protestors organized a sit-in in Tahrir Square in Cairo to demand the criminal prosecution of Mubarak and other regime officials, cancel anti-protest laws, and the dissolution of local councils.

On 30 September 2011, hundreds of protesters gathered in front of the Ministry of Defence in Kubri al Qubbah in Cairo to denounce the SCAFs new electoral law and to demand the end of the state of emergency. The police deployed reinforcements in the area and cordoned off the building to prevent protesters from storming the building.

On Feb 27 or Feb 28 (unclear), in front of the Ministry of Education in Abdeen, Cairo, around 2,000 high school students demanded a diminution of their education program as well as to delay their exams' period.

On 18 December 2011, dozens of political activists demonstrated in front of the Journalists' Syndicate in the Al Azbakiyya area of Cairo to denounce the use of violence by police and army units and to demand the overthrow of the military rule of the country.

On 2 June 2011, the American University in Tahrir Square witnessed a gathering of hundreds of people wishing to meet Dr. Ahmed Zewail, but they were surprised by the security of the university preventing them from entering due to the lack of places. They held a protest in front of the gate and expressed their demand to attend and participate in the seminar.

On 22 June 2011, a number of temporary workers at the Desert Research Centers under the Ministry of Agriculture, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, to demand Dr. Essam Sharaf and Dr. Ayman Farid Abu Hadid, Minister of Agriculture to appoint temporary workers. Dozens of imams excluded by the former state security, protested in front of the Council of Ministers, to demand Dr. Essam Sharaf, and Minister of Awqaf Abdullah al-Husseini, immediate re-appointment.

On 28 December 2011, dozens of families of those killed in protests in the past year demonstrated in front of a police academy in New Cairo-1 area of Cairo where former president Hosni Mubarak, his sons, and other associates were being put on trial.

On 21 June 2011, the Egyptian Family Protection Coalition, and the Egyptian Men's Revolution Movement, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, to ask Prime Minister Dr. Essam Sharaf, and Minister of Justice, to drop the family laws issued under the former president, which was requested by his wife.
On 18 May 2011, dozens of veterinarians staged a protest in front of the General Veterinaries Syndicate in Garden City to protest the postponement of the Syndicate's council to hold elections.

On 7 June 2011, dozens of employees of the Egyptian Company for Electricity Transmission held a sit-in at the Ministry of Electricity and Energy, demanding that Dr. Hassan Younis, Minister of Electricity, pay the cumulative incentives promised during his meeting with them last April.

On 7 June 2011, dozens of Al Salam camp demonstrators blocked the Nile Corniche, and confrontations between demonstrators and car drivers breaking out. Police forces intervened to negotiate with the demonstrators to break up the sit-in.

On 26 June 2011, a group of 'anti-corruption watchers' organized a protest in front of the Central Auditing Organization on Salah Salem Street in Nasr City, to demand the dismissal of the head of the organization as part of the former regime.

On 21 May 2011, dozens of supporters of the former president staged a protest in front of the Radio and TV building, to support the military council and the armed forces, rejecting any presidential council of any kind, and demanding to release the former president with any guarantees the prosecution deems, as no evidence against him has so far been proven.

During 4 days, in Maadi, between Feb 10 - Feb 13, hundreds of employees from the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency staged a sit-in in front of the Agency's headquarters to demand better working conditions and higher salaries. (coded as 4 events)

On 19 June 2011, journalists started a sit-in at the office of Deputy Prime Minister Dr. Yahya al-Jamal in protest against the failure to implement the decision issued by the Council of Ministers to assign them to national newspapers.
On 9 December 2011, dozens of demonstrators marched from Tahrir Square to Parliament with coffins symbolizing those lost during the Egyptian revolution.

On 15 June 2011, dozens of journalists staged a protest in front of the Journalists Syndicate in Qasr Al-Nile, to denounce the attack on the Coptic TV channel reporter 'Mary' while covering the recent demonstrations in Tahrir Square.

On 27 June 2011, dozens of young people, with the participation of the Free Egypt Revolution Coalition, gathered in front of the Spanish Embassy in El Gezra, to demand the revocation of Spanish citizenship of the fugitive businessman Hussein Salem, and hand him over to Egypt after recovering the money he looted from the Egyptian people.

On 15 April 2011, around 600 Mubarak supporters demonstrated in front of the state TV and radio building against putting him on trial and to denounce perceived insults directed towards the former Egyptian leader. Later, tens of demonstrators demanding that Mubarak stand trial marched to the state TV and radio building and clashed with the pro-Mubarak supporters. Military forces intervened to disperse the demonstrators and end the clashes.

On 22 December 2011, hundreds of students marched from Ain Shams University in Al Wayli area of Cairo to pay tribute to a political activist killed in protests the day prior. The students also called for the military council to step down immediately.
On 11 May 2011, Copts demonstrating in front of the Radio and TV building refused to negotiate with security leaders to open the road for traffic, until their legitimate demands are met.

On 04 April 2011, more than 150 protestors held a sit-in in Tahrir Square in Cairo to demand criminal prosecution of Mubarak and other regime officials, the cancelling anti-protest laws, and the dissolution of local councils.

On 23 May 2011, the Syrian community in Cairo, rejecting Bashar Al Assad's stay as president of Syria, organized a silent protest in Al-Nozha, to condemn the crimes committed by Assad against the demonstrators who demanded his downfall.

On 28 June 2011, in a rapid development of events in front of Al Azhar in El-Gamaleya rioters sat on the ground in front of the main doors of the building, making it difficult for the leaders of Al Azhar and the members of the Islamic Research Complex to leave. Security forces intervened.

On 17 December 2011, students and other protesters demonstrated in front of Al-Azhar University in Nasr City district of Cairo demanding retribution for the killing of Sheikh Emad Effat and the rest who were killed in protests in front of the Council of Ministers .

On 5 June 2011, traffic on the Magles Al Shab Street in front of the Council of Ministers witnessed severe turbulence after a number of protesters staged three protests in front of the Council of Ministers. Dozens of license holders suspended at the Ministry of Petroleum staged a demand for immediate appointment, denouncing their disapproval.
On 17 August 2011, a demonstration organized by a number of Salafi groups took place in front of Nour Mosque in Al Abbasiyah in Cairo denouncing the proposed Supra-Constitutional Principles document.
On 18 June 2011, a number of NDP supporters of former Minister of Housing Ahmed al-Maghrabi staged a protest in front of the New Cairo Court at the Fifth Settlement in New Cairo City.

During 4 days, in Maadi, between Feb 10 - Feb 13, hundreds of employees from the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency staged a sit-in in front of the Agency's headquarters to demand better working conditions and higher salaries. (coded as 4 events)

On 23 May 2011, a large number of those injured in the January 25 revolution, staged a protest in Tahrir Square, in solidarity with the January 25 Youth Coalition, demanding the honoring of the wounded of the revolution, and their involvement in the national talks, and the suspension of military officers and police accused of assaulting protesters.

During 4 days, in Maadi, between Feb 10 - Feb 13, hundreds of employees from the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency staged a sit-in in front of the Agency's headquarters to demand better working conditions and higher salaries. (coded as 4 events)
On 18 May 2011, dozens of archaeological workers renewed their demonstration before the Council of Ministers to demand permanent work

On 14 May 2011, hundreds of young people gathered in Tahrir Square, from different governorates, in preparation of going to Rafah to participate in breaking the Israeli siege on the Gaza Strip, declaring their financial and moral support to the people of Gaza. They marched towards the Israeli embassy in Al Maadi joing others to demand the expulsion of the ambassador and lowering the flag from the embassy and eventually burned the Israeli flag.

On 29 May 2011, a number of Giza lawyers organized a protest in front of the Prosecutor General Office in Al Rehab, to denounce the difficulties and suffering they find while working in courts and prosecutors, during investigations and mistreatment by prosecutors.

On 20 June 2011, nearly 50 workers from Telemisr, a manufacturer of electrical devices, protested in front of the office of Minister of Manpower and Immigration Dr. Ahmed El-Borai in Nasr City, to demand their return to work and restart the company at full capacity.

assoc_actor_1: CYR: Coalition of the Youth of the Revolution
notes: On 29 December 2011, dozens of demonstrators including Coalition of the Youth of the Revolution members protested in Sayyida Zeinab area of Cairo against the acquittal of a police officer accused of wounding and killing protesters.

On 30 April 2011, thousands of people, including members of Al-Gamaa al-Islamiyya, gathered in front of Al-Nour Mosque in Abbasiya and headed towards the The Supreme Council of the Armed Forces building to demand the release of political prisoners who were arrested and subjected to military trials during the Mubarak era.

On 3 November 2011, a march commemorating the slain activist Mina Daniel arrived at the Ramses Square in the Shubra junction in the Al Sahil neighborhood of Cairo district. The marchers, which consisted of a large group of Coptic Egyptians, demanded the release of detainees from military prisons, the resignation of the Information Minister, and prosecution for those responsible for the deaths at Maspero.

On 9 November 2011, Syrian demonstrators blocked the entrance to the headquarters of the Arab League in Tahrir Square and prevented a number of representatives from Syrian revolutionary councils from entering.
On 3 November 2011, dozens of Ghad Political Party supporters held a vigil in the Talaat Harb Square in solidarity with their detained leader, Ayman Nour.

On 22 May 2011, nearly 500 temporary workers at Petrojet, staged a protest in front of the headquarters of the Ministry of Petroleum in Nasr city, to demand their return to their company, after they were laid off after several projects were stopped.
On 15 June 2011, a number of disabled workers in Ezz Dekheila Company in Alexandria continued to demonstrate in front of the Council of Ministers demanding equality.

On 1 November 2011, a group of roughly 25 journalists protested in front of the Egyptian parliament in the Haidaq al-Qubba neighborhood of Cairo against a Deputy Prime Minister for Political Development and Democratic Transformation, calling the individual corrupt.
On 13 May 2011, Coptic demonstrators marched from Ghamrah towards the TV building, to join the protesters.
On 30 December 2011, dozens of demonstrators protested in Sayyida Zeinab area of Cairo against the acquittal of a police officer accused of wounding and killing protesters.

On 20 June 2011, a number of contract workers at Al Ahram specialized hospital, held a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, demanding that Dr. Essam Sharaf, and Dr. Ashraf Hatem, Minister of Health let them return to work.

On 17 May 2011, around 50 university professors from the Unified Coalition of Egyptian University Staff, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers to demand the dismissal of the Minister of Higher Education Dr. Amr Ezzat Salama.

On 21 December 2011, dozens of mourners marched from Tahrir Square in Cairo to the Zeinhoum morgue to condemn the killing of a political activist killed in protests in the Square several days prior. Later the same day, 3,000 demonstrators from over 26 different Islamic and political groups marched from Tahrir Square to protest the violent response by government forces to the protests in Tahrir Square over the weekend.

On 29 May 2011, parents of students of the Egyptian Nile schools affiliated with the Council of Ministers held a protest in front of the Council headquarters in Cairo, after they were informed of the possibility of transferring school dependency from the Education Development Fund of the Council of Ministers to the Ministry of Education. Nearly 200 members of the Egyptian Boy Scouts, starting from the heads of the 6-year-old Central Boy Scouts Association in Egypt, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers to demand the departure of the President of the National Council for Youth Mohamed Safi El-Din Kharboush. Owners of the Egyptian labor companies in Saudi Arabia blocked Magles Al Shab street, more than once in protest against what they called the 'monopoly' of the Ministry of Manpower for the procedures of transferring Egyptian workers to Saudi Arabia during the coming Hajj season.
On 20 November 2011, Muslim Brotherhood students gathered and chanted slogans against the military outside Helwan University in Cairo.

On 27 November 2011, a group of students marched from Cairo University to meet the sit-in taking place in front of the Council of Ministers building next to Tahrir Square. The protesters called for an immediate handover to a civilian-led government.

On 24 May 2011, nearly 150 preachers from Al-Azhar Coalition staged a march that began after noon prayers from Al Nour Mosque in Al Abbasiyah, towards the Military Council, to demand the independence and unity of Al-Azhar, and declaring an open sit-in until all their demands are met.

On 1 November 2011, a group of families of individuals killed on January 25th of that year carried out a protest vigil in front of the Welfare Building in the As-Sayyidah Zainab area of Cairo, calling for a speedy prosecution of those responsible for the killings.

On 10 June 2011, nearly 300 Salafists staged a march from Omar Makram Mosque in Qasr Ad Dobarah, to demand the release of Abu Yahya, who was arrested pending an investigation into the recent events in Imbabah. The march went to TV Building, through Tahrir Square.

On 17 June 2011, a number of disabled employees of Ezz Dekheila company in Alexandria moved their demonstration today to Tahrir Square, to demand equality. Another 500 protesters gathered in the square, which led to traffic disruption, and many expressed their opposition to talk about the corruption of sports, stressing that the main demands of the revolution and demonstrations are the urgent trial of Mubarak and his people, and the dissolution of local councils.
On 19 May 2011, hundreds staged a demonstration in front of the US Embassy in Garden City to demand the release of Sheikh Omar Abdul Rahman, who has been held in US jails for 18 years.

On 8 June 2011, four demonstrations occurred in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, organized by temporary employees of the Desert Research Center, first graduates of Al Azhar University, employees of Al-Nasr Company, and farmers, blocking the road till their demands are met. Dozens were arrested after the police forces intervened.

On 5 June 2011, traffic on the Magles Al Shab Street in front of the Council of Ministers witnessed severe turbulence after a number of protesters staged three protests in front of the Council of Ministers. Dozens of agricultural research workers of the Ministry of Agriculture held a stand in front of the Council of Ministers to demand Dr. Essam Sharaf to appoint temporary workers who have been working for five years.

On 6 June 2011, hundreds of the first graduates of Al Azhar University announced their participation in an open sit-in in front of the Council of Ministers, because their demands for appointment were ignored by the ministry.
On 1 June 2011, dozens of employees of the Egyptian Electricity Transmission Company held a sit-in in front of the Ministry of Electricity and Energy's office in Abbasiya.
On 13 May 2011, a marches from Muqattam, was directed at the Corniche and the headquarters of the Radio and TV Building to join the protesters in condemning the events of Imbabah.

On 27 June 2011, the families of martyrs and those injured in the January 25 revolution, continued their demonstration in front of the TV Building in As Sabtiyyah, and refused to open the road, insisting that it should not be opened until their demands are met, which included the urgent trial of Mubarak and his aides publicly.

On 27 June 2011, dozens of residents of Kafr ash Shaykh, governorate gathered in front of the Foreign Affairs Ministry building in Bulaq, demanding the return of their relatives from Libyan territories.

On 26 June 2011, in response to the decision to postpone the trial of Al Adly until July 25th, the families of martyrs and wounded from the revolution demonstrated in front of the Radio and TV Building in As Sabtiyyah, by blocking the road refusing the decision to postpone the trial.
On 21 June 2011, about 20 Egyptian activists gathered in front of the Spanish embassy in El Gezra, to demand Spanish authorities extradite Egyptian businessman Hussein Salem.

On 28 June 2011, a number of temporary workers in specialized gardens, staged a protest in Al Qasr al Ayni Street in front of the Council of Ministers, to demand that Dr. Essam Sharaf, Prime Minister, appoint the workers. Imams also continued their sit-in in front of the Council of Ministers.

On 27 June 2011, Al Qasr al Ayni Street experienced a demonstration with drums, dance and national songs, to force officials to hear the voices of protesters who were prevented by the Central Security Forces from entering the Magles Al Shab Street. The families of the martyr Mahmoud Khaled Qatb closed Al Qasr al Ayni road, protested against delay of his body for more than 3 hours. Verbal altercations occurred between parents and cars' drivers.
On 15 June 2011, dozens of the General Coalition of Police Secretaries, staged a protest at Tahrir Square, to demand the abolition of military courts and arbitrary transfers.

On 23 June 2011, Qasr Al-Aini Street experienced a traffic disruption after a number of temporary workers at the specialized sports medical centers of the National Sports Council, organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, to demand Dr. Essam Sharaf appoint them immediately.

On 6 November 2011, dozens of Muslims who were praying during Eid al-Adha marched to Tahrir Square following their prayer and called for a halt to military courts, the release of political prisoners, and a swift transition to civilian rule in the country.

On 13 May 2019, for the sixth consecutive day, thousands of demonstrators flocked to the Radio and TV building, and large numbers from all governorates poured in solidarity with the protesting Coptic youth, demanding national unity and holding perpetrators accountable for the recent events in Imbabah. 22 coalition, movement, front and association representing different national and local entities. Copts confronted four people coming from the direction of the Corniche, over their demand to break up the sit-in in front of the TV building, while security forces broke up the clash and took the four away from the area.
On 6 May 2011, a large number of real estate owners staged a protest in front of Radio and TV building in As Sabtiyyah area, demanding the cancellation of the inheritance system.

On 18 June 2011, the Council of Ministers Street witnessed three protests by people demanding jobs, including dozens of employees of the legal departments of the government, who organized a silent protest to demand the establishment of a public authority for the legal department of the government. A number of employees of the National Sports Council also organized a rally, demanding jobs. A third group of disabled persons in Ezz Al-Dekheila Company, resumed their sit-in in front of the Council to demand equality.

On 19 June 2011, three protests over employment were organized in front of the Council of Ministers, initiated by holders of suspended Licenses at the Ministry of Petroleum, demanding the prime minister, to immediately appoint holders of suspended licenses. A number of families in the eastern district of Shubra Dakahlia, which their old real estate was removed, gathered demanding Dr. Essam Sharaf access to housing units.
On 20 May 2011, hundreds of citizens held a stand to support the ruling military council in front of the unidentified soldier's monument in Nasr City.

On 20 May 2011, more than 2000 Muslims gathered in front of Almaza underwear factory in Ain Shams, after Friday prayers at Al Nour mosque facing the factory, stressing that they will not allow the conversion of the factory into a church.
On 07 April 2011, more than 400 protestors organized a sit-in in Tahrir Square in Cairo to demand criminal prosecution of Mubarak and other regime officials.

On 23 November 2011, rioters clashed with police on Al Bustan Street in Talaat Harb Square with demonstrators erecting iron barriers to try and prevent security forces from entering nearby Tahrir Square. Dozens of rioters were wounded by tear gas and rubber bullets.

On 2 June 2011, a march started in Tahrir Square with 300 independent young people heading to the Council of Ministers, in support of the second revolution of anger demanding Dr. Essam Sharaf to prosecute the symbols of corruption and the failure of the security authorities in decision-making.

On 28 May 2011, dozens of demonstrators continued their protest for the second day in Tahrir Square. The protesters have set up tents to emphasize the demands of the second revolution of anger, which include many new demands, most notably the execution of Mubarak and Habib Al Adly and rapid public trials.

On 6 May 2011, around 1,000 Coptic protesters gathered outside the Cathedral of St. Mark in Al Abbasiyah, and the same inside the cathedral, in response to the Salafists' demonstration in front of the cathedral last Friday. The demonstration came amid an intense security presence. A number of Muslims joined the Coptic demonstration and chanted for national unity.

On 3 June 2011, the coalition of workers in tourism and hotels organized a demonstration in Tahrir Square after Friday prayers, to demand the fixation of temporary workers, especially those that have been working more than three years, like the decision of the Minister of Finance Samir Radwan. Nearly 1,000 demonstrators gathered as well to emphasize the demands of the revolution, which include the prosecution of symbols of corruption, and accelerate their trials, the issuance of a new constitution governing the country and the execution of Mubarak. Dozens of Tahrir Square demonstrators attacked the Coptic reporter for CTV channel during her coverage of Tahrir Square demonstrations on Friday, the security forces saved her by firing shots in the air to disperse the aggressors, and they put her inside a car to escape from Tahrir Square. The demonstrators also attacked the officer who fired shots in the air, and took his gun.

On 12 November 2011, a group of recently fired police secretaries stormed past the police security cordon at the Egyptian parliament in the Haidaq Al-Qubba neighborhood of Cairo and demanded that they be reinstated in their jobs.

On 4 May 2011, a demonstration by the Abu Ghattas workshop workers at the Ramses station occupied the tracks, disrupting service, to demand the establishment of an emergency ambulance unit for use in emergencies.

On 13 May 2011, thousands of protesters gathered at Tahrir Square, to march and support the Palestinian cause on its first day in the third Intifada. Some of the demonstrators joined protesters in front of the TV building.

On 24 June 2011, demonstrators of Tahrir Square burned the Israeli flag denouncing the crimes committed by Israel against the Palestinians, demanding a boycott of American goods, because they support Israel in its sabotage and they threatened to burn the American flag.

On 15 May 2011, clashes between Coptic demonstrators and a group of rioters were renewed near the Radio and TV building, and Molotov cocktails were thrown at the Coptic demonstrators. The two sides exchanged rock-throwing, causing injuries among demonstrator and material damage. Police and army forces intervened to confront the aggressors and stop the violence. Gunfire was fired, injuring at least four Copts. Police fired tear gas to counter the groups who attacked the demonstrators. Demonstrators also attacked one of the cameramen, who was there to cover the event.

On 1 May 2011, dozens of information center employees gathered in Tahrir Square and then headed to the Council of Ministers in Al-Qasr Al-Aini Street to protest against the slow transfer of their funds by the Ministry of Finance, and accused officials of various ministries of manipulating them.

On 23 May 2011, dozens of Fathers of Al Roya blocked Salah Salem's road in Nasr city, to demonstrate against the decision of the Islamic Research Academy to postpone consideration of amending family laws indefinitely.

On 13 May 2011, activists started a march from Nasr City towards the Rafah border in solidarity with Palestinians. The security forces intervened to disperse the protesters and arrested a number of them.
On 4 May 2011, dozens of temporary employees of the Atomic Energy Organization continued their protest at the Aman nuclear center, to demand their contracts be adjusted.

On 25 December 2011, dozens of students and Ultras Zamalek football fans demonstrated in front of the high court in Ain Shams area of Cairo and demanded the release of those detained in connection with recent protests.

On 20 November 2011, police at Cairo International Airport broke up a clash between supporters and opponents of the Bashar al Assad regime that took place as a plane form Syria landed at the airport. Neither injuries nor fatalities were reported.

On 10 June 2011, dozens of demonstrators in Tahrir Square, after Friday prayers, raised banners calling for the completion of the demands of the revolution, including the demand for the execution of the former president and the restructuring of the police. They also assaulted a police secretary named Adel A. after shouting slogans calling for restructuring the Ministry of Interiors. Dozens of teachers staged a demonstration in Tahrir Square as well, demanding the formation of a teachers' parliament that will join experts and officials in formulating educational policy.

On 1 November 2011, lawyers protested outside the Egyptian Bar Association building in the Qasr Al-Nile neighborhood of Cairo against the President of the Association and called for a purge of the judiciary and for their involvement in monitoring the upcoming elections.
On 13 May 2011, Coptic demonstrators marched from Shubra towards TV building, to join the protesters in condemning the events of Imbabah.

On 17 June 2011, dozens of Salafists gathered in front of Al Nour Mosque in Al Abbasiyah, to demand the release of Salafi activist Moftah Mohamed Fadel 'Abu Yahya', detained for inciting sedition on the events of sectarian strife in Imbaba.

On 24 June 2011, dozens of families of the martyrs and wounded of the revolution started a sit-in in front of the TV Building in As Sabtiyyah to demand the inclusion of former president and Anas al-Fiqi, Tariq Kamel and Hatem al-Jabali in the list of suspects in the case of killing and injuring the rebels, they were also blocking and opening the road regularly.

On 19 June 2011, three protests over employment were organized in front of the Council of Ministers, initiated by holders of suspended Licenses at the Ministry of Petroleum, demanding the prime minister, to immediately appoint holders of suspended licenses. Also a number of disabled workers in Ezz Dekheila Company in Alexandria continue to demonstrate for the seventh day in front of the Council of Ministers, demanding Field Marshal Mohamed Hussein Tantawi and Dr. Essam Sharaf equality.

On 8 December 2011, a group of protesters demonstrated near the Kasr al Ain Hospital in Hadaiq Al-Qubba district of Cairo in solidarity with those who lost their sight during demonstrations in the country.
On 12 June 2011, dozens of parents of Al-Azhar students who failed the exams protested in front of Al-Azhar in El-Gamaleya.
On 26 December 2011, dozens of demonstrators held a silent march around the Al Abbasiyah area of Cairo in solidarity with the Tahrir Square protesters.
On 22 June 2011, nearly 200 Pro-Mubarak Supporters, protested in front of Abdeen court.

On 3 June 2011, a number of families of those injured in the clashes in front of Al-Azbakiya Police Station gathered in front of Al Hilal Hospital, and threw stones at the hospital. Security forces intervened.

On 25 May 2011, dozens of veterinarians staged a protest in front of the General Veterinaries Syndicate in Garden City, demanding the departure of the board of the union and threatened to organize an open sit-in if the union council did not respond to their various demands, especially the holding of elections according to the decisions of the General Assembly on July 30 of this year.

During 4 days, in Maadi, between Feb 10 - Feb 13, hundreds of employees from the Egyptian Environmental Affairs Agency staged a sit-in in front of the Agency's headquarters to demand better working conditions and higher salaries. (coded as 4 events)

On 10 April 2011, more than 100 demonstrators gathered in Tahrir Square demanding the opening of the square and the removal of barricades and barbed wire. Demonstrators demanding the removal of the barricaded and those who constructed it threw strones at each other. The barricade had been constructed by demonstrators demanding the prosecution of Mubarak and members of his government, who were accused of causing frictions between the people and the army.

assoc_actor_1: Fishers (Egypt); Labor Group (Egypt); Women (Egypt); Government of Egypt (2011-2012)
notes: On 13 June 2011, a group of fishers from Izbat al Burj in Damietta governorate organized a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, demanding the Prime Minister Dr. Essam Sharaf, and Dr. Ayman Farid Abu Hadid, Minister of Agriculture, exclude fishing hooks users from the decision to ban fishing in the Mediterranean, because it doesn't harm the sea. 22 female workers in the Ministry of Family and Population in the Family Friends Program in Kafr El-Sheikh, held a protest in front of the Council of Ministers in Cairo, calling on Prime Minister Dr. Essam Sharaf to appoint their jobs. Also, dozens of disabled employees of Ezz Dekheila Company continued to demonstrate for the second day in front of the Council of Ministers, demanding Dr. Essam Sharaf to treat the disabled employees like others working in the company and raise their basic salary.

On 12 December 2011, a number of Kifaya Movement members yelled slogans of support in front of the High Court in the Maadi district of Cairo. Passerbys got into verbal altercations with the group, claiming they do not represent all Egyptians.

On 31 May 2011, the police broke up the sit-in of about 40 imams which lasted for three days in front of the office of the Minister of Endowments, Abdullah Husseini Hilal in Abdeen, due to preventing the minister from practicing his work, and disrupting the work of the ministry.

On 6 May 2011, dozens of Egyptian activists and the Preparatory Committee for the third Palestinian Intifada, organized a demonstration outside the Israeli embassy in Al Maadi, to demand the expulsion of the Israeli ambassador and prepare for the third Palestinian uprising, scheduled for May 15. They called for an end to Egypt's export of gas to Israel, and demanded the evacuation of the Israeli Embassy, and burned about ten Israeli flags in front of the embassy. More than 200 demonstrators from Tahrir square joined them as well. They also blocked the road at the top of the university bridge in front of the embassy. Security forces gathered to protect the embassy from storming by demonstrators.

On 7 June 2011, dozens of citizens from the 'I am Egyptian with the Intifada' movement staged a protest in front of the Israeli embassy in Al Maadi to denounce the crimes against the Palestinian people.

On 2 November 2011, dozens of members of a group calling itself Movement for All Unemployed demonstrated in front of the Prime Minister's office in the Haidaq Al-Qubba neighborhood of Cairo. The group's members threatened collective suicide if the government did not provide them with public sector jobs.

On 15 November 2011, dozens of workers from the Misr Fertilizers Production Company (MOPCO) protested in front of the Egyptian parliament it in the Haidaq Al-Qubba neighborhood of Cairo against the closure of a fertilizer plant in Damietta. A protest in Diametta several days prior had turned deadly.
On 14 June 2011, A number of disabled workers in Ezz Dekheila Company in Alexandria continued to demonstrate in front of the Council of Ministers demanding equality.

On 14 June 2011, a march of dozens of veterinarians was launched in front of Dar Al Hikma in Al Qasr al Ayni, towards the Council of Ministers to deliver a memorandum containing a series of demands, the first of which is an independent veterinary entity under the Council of Ministers.

On 11 May 2011, in front of the Council of Ministers in Al Qasr al Ayni, Cairo, dozens of shopkeepers in the new cities protested against the decision of Dr. Fathi ElBaradei, Minister of Housing, Utilities and Urban Development, to renew the temporary contracts for their shops for only three years, after which they will leave the shops completely.

On 6 May 2011, the movement 'I am Egyptian with the Intifada' organized a march that emerged from Omar Makram Mosque after Friday prayers in Tahrir Square, which included more than 300 demonstrators in support of the Palestinian uprising on May 15. They then headed through Al Qasr al Ayni road to the Israeli Embassy to demonstrate in front of it. The Coalition of Arab People, together with the Youth Revolution Coalition, organized a march in Tahrir Square in solidarity with the Arab peoples and their revolutions. The demonstrators held a 10 meter flag bearing the flags of the various Arab countries. The march was joined by a number of Syrians and Libyans present in the square. They condemned the massacres committed by Bashar al-Assad against the rebels. During their tour in Tahrir Square, more than 500 Egyptian demonstrators and dozens of Libyans joined the Syrians, whom also condemned the massacres committed by Gaddafi.

News reports say scores of youths blocked road connecting Al-Mansurah to Jamasah to protest Interior Ministrys 'violence' towards demonstrators.

Residents of the governorates of Qena, Aswan and Al-Sharqeya blocked railways and roads on Sunday morning in protest over the fuel shortage. Groups in Zagazig, Al-Sharqeya, also blocked the railroad for some hours. Yasser Al-Refaei, a Revolutionary Youth Coalition representative in Al-Sharqeya, said the groups resolved the blockage after negotiations with governmental officials. Two families quarrelled over their places in a long queue at a gas station, which then escalated into violent fighting. Both families used weapons, leaving two people dead and five others injured.

Protests broke out in Mansoura city on Sunday in reaction to the violent clashes the city has witnessed during recent days. The clashes began when mourners threw rocks at security forces located at one of Mansouras prisons, as they passed by the prison on their way from Al-Nasr mosque towards the Abdel Azeem families cemetery. Security forces fired cautionary gunshots in the air and clashes ended as mourners moved on towards the cemetery.Hours later, violent clashes again broke out between hundreds of protesters gathered at Al-Thawra Square and CSF members. Protesters hurled rocks at the CSF who fired an extensive amount of teargas bombs and birdshot

Massive demonstration takes place in Al-Daqahliyah Governorate to denounce acts of violence. Demonstrators criticize security men's way of dealing with protesters, call for dissolving current government.

After days of protests and strikes conducted by police warrant officers, Minister of Interior Mohamed Ibrahim agreed to 19 of the protesters demands.The nationwide strike primarily demanded the repeal of the protest law passed by the Shura Council, Egypts upper house of parliament. The new protest law bans military and police personnel from engaging in any acts of protest, which includes strikes and sit-ins. The striking policemen also demanded the removal of the Minister of Interior from the cabinet. Dozens are participating in the strike in Assiut, Luxor, Aswan and North Sinai while hundreds did so in Monufia and Qalyubia. Several cities, including Mansoura and Beni Suef, saw policemen temporarily suspend their sit-in to give authorities time to respond to their demands.

Police responded with tear gas, water cannons, rubber bullets, and live ammunition in an effort to prevent protesters from advancing towards the central squares of those cities. The Ministry of Health said 846 persons died during the protests in January and February. Most of these were killed on January 28 and 29.

Police responded with tear gas, water cannons, rubber bullets, and live ammunition in an effort to prevent protesters from advancing towards the central squares of those cities. The Ministry of Health said 846 persons died during the protests in January and February. Most of these were killed on January 28 and 29.
