Police responded with tear gas, water cannons, rubber bullets, and live ammunition in an effort to prevent protesters from advancing towards the central squares of those cities. The Ministry of Health said 846 persons died during the protests in January and February. Most of these were killed on January 28 and 29.

actor2: Military Forces of Egypt (2011-2012) Military Police
notes: On 2 August 2011, hundreds of protesters gathered in Tahrir Square to denounce the dispersion of the sit-in by the military police the previous day. The military police cordoned off the Square's garden and deployed hundreds of police officers to the area to prevent another sit-in.
Demonstrators stormed the NDP HQ in the Tanta governorate.

assoc_actor_1: Bedouin Ethnic Group (Egypt); Al Fouakhriya Tribal Group (Egypt); Muslim Group (Egypt)
notes: On 25 May 2011, the city of Al Arish, the capital of North Sinai, witnessed a large demonstration following calls to gather thousands from Al-Nasr, Al-Rifai and Al-Fawakhriya mosques to the Diwan of the governorate to stage a sit-in and close Al Arish. The Al-Fawakhriyah tribe gathered with all its families to face the phenomenon of kidnapping individuals, stealing cars, and terrorizing citizens.

assoc_actor_1: Bedouin Ethnic Group (Egypt); Al Fouakhriya Tribal Group (Egypt)
notes: On 24 May 2011, dozens of Al-Fawakhriyah tribal youths in Al-Arish blocked the international road of Al-Qantara Al-Arish to demonstrate against the disappearance of nine members of the tribe, who were allegedly abducted by the Tayaha tribe, which alleges that the Al-Fawakhrya kidnapped one of their members.

On 8 June 2012, a group led by the National Coordination Authority gathered at the Al-Qa'ed Ibrahim mosque in Al Attarin (Al Atareen, Alexandria) and protest demanding the retrial of former president Mubarak and his relatives based on new evidence.

On 11 June 2011, more than 20 youths of the Tagammu Party entered an open sit-in inside the party's central headquarters in Qasr Al-Nile, in protest against the inclusion of former leaders from the National Party to the Tagammu party's membership during the past months.

assoc_actor_1: EFITU: Egyptian Federation of Independent Trade Unions; Labor Group (Egypt)
notes: On 31 October 2011, dozens of members of the Egyptian Federation of Independent Trade Unions and a number of candidates on the workers seat, organized a protest in front of 6th of October Elementary Court building in Giza Square, Giza, to denounce the refusal of the examination committee in the court to accept their candidacy papers on the seats of workers in the parliament.

assoc_actor_1: Egyptian Socialist Party; SPAP: Socialist Popular Alliance Party; Egyptian Communist Party
notes: A number of political movements participated in a march to express their rejection of the International Monetary Funds (IMF) loan
At least two political parties have announced that they will be protesting outside the Shura Council announcing their rejection of the councils discussion of proposed amendments to the judiciary law.
Protesters marched from Sayeda Zeinab Square to Tahrir Square on labour day to protest labour reform
200 protesters gathered in central Cairo on Tuesday to demand that women's rights be protected in the new constitution

On 21 February 2011, About 100 truck drivers protesters demonstrated in the port-town of Adabiya (Ataka, Suez) about rising tolls in the area and alleged mistreatment by the ports administration. Military forces arrived and detained at least 5 protesters. A woman was allegedly killed by a tank as she attempted to stop them from taking away her son. The protest swelled to about 1,000 people after news of her death spread.

On 18 November 2012, at least four people were dead and more were suspected missing after the army attacked a group of protesters who had blocked roads in a longstanding land dispute in Al Jizah (Giza, Giza).
Ministry of Tourism employees protested outside the ministry in Cairo at the appointment of the new governor of Luxor, who they denounced as a member of an extremist organisation.

A few hundred people gathered at the presidential palace in Heliopolis for an anti-government protest after President Mubaraks speech saying he wouldnt step down. Military forces guarded the palace with barbed wire.
Hundreds of Egyptian police protest alleging corruption in their ranks, including order to shoot anti-Mubarak protesters.
On 11 February 2011, in Port Said, demonstrators reacted angrily to Mubarak's speech. Gunshots were fired by police to disperse the protesters (unclear if Police shot at protesters).

On 11 February 2011, in Tima, rioters demonstrated against Mubarak and tried to burn down the National Party's headquarters. They clashed with Police Forces, throwing rocks at them. Police Forces shot several times at the crowd and used tear gas against the demonstrators. No fatalities reported.
On 11 February 2011, a female CBS journalist was sexually assaulted and beaten before being saved by a group of woman and soldiers.

On 10 December 2011, some thugs in Tahrir Square attempted to close the square again to traffic and attacked and smashed some cars which led to altercations with other people in the square. Neither injuries nor fatalities were reported.

On 15 December 2011, army forces broke up a clash between Al-Nour Party supporters and supporters of an independent candidate in the village of Al Sanjara in Sharkia governorate after the latter accused the Al-Nour Party of illegally dealing with and contacting voters in the area. Neither injuries nor fatalities were reported during the scuffle.

On 15 December 2011, army forces fired over the heads of demonstrators outside the Imam al-Busairi school in Bulaq district of Cairo. The demonstrators were attempting to storm the school which was being used as a polling station.

On 21 December 2011, hundreds of demonstrators marched in front of the American Embassy in Qasr al Nile area of Cairo and mourned the death of a political activist killed in the recent riots in Tahrir Square. The rioters chanted slogans and threw rocks at the American embassy.

On 31 December 2011, residents of the village of Abu Ghalib set fire to the Orascom Company equipment and facilities in the Manshiyat al Qanatir area of Giza governorate to protest the establishment of a power station in their village that they believed to be a nuclear power plant which was, in fact, not the case.

actor2: Police Forces of Egypt (2011-2012) Prison Guards
notes: At least 6 police were injured in a prison riot at el-Qanater on the outskirts of Cairo. At least 9 inmates were injured when the guards opened fire to disperse the riot.

Hundreds of Copts from Al-Kamader village in Minya protested in front of the governorate building to demand that a service center be transformed into a church. This led a group of Muslims to besiege the facility.
Around 500 protesters in Sohag blocked a railroad to Aswan over repeated quarrels between two tribes in the area. They demanded greater police protection.

Hundreds of protesters remained barricaded in Tahrir Square and said they will continue their demonstration until Mubarak and his family are put on trial and a civil presidential council is created. A minor scuffle with soldiers was reported in one part of the Square but other protesters separated the two groups. Two groups of protesters also got into a scuffle over attempts to remove the barbed wire, while others threw stones at vehicles attempting to enter the Square.

An army officer and a group of men with him stormed a building in the Imbaba area where a conference organized by a FB group 'No to Trials for Civilians before Military Courts'. A scuffle broke out after the group burst into the hall, shouting that foreigners and journalists were present. At least one person was injured and 3 later arrested by police.

Hundreds of family members and friends of a man who had been killed surrounded the hospital where his allegedly killer was being treated and eventually broke in and killed the wounded man. Fighting was also reported on and around the premises.

On 3 May 2011, a number of employees of the Ministry of Finance and members of the security of the ministry attacked Mona Zia, editor of 'Day Seven', during the coverage of the strike by a number of junior employees of the minister's office, the technical office, and a number of sectors of the ministry, to demonstrate against the deduction of their salaries. Three female employees of the ministry tied the hands of the editor and took the papers she was carrying demanding security to expel her outside the ministry.
On 17 May 2011, there were physical confrontations between a number of Copts demonstrating in front of the Radio and TV building over the suspension of one of the demonstrators.
On 15 June 2011, in Minya, Coptic Christians demonstrated, demanding authorities search for two disappeared Christian girls, three policemen and six Coptic demonstrators were injured.

On 26 June 2011, a number of temporary workers in sports medical centers organized a demonstration to demand Prime Minister Dr. Essam Sharaf appointment them, which led to traffic disruption in Al Qasr al Ayni Street, and confrontations between them and the policemen because they objected to blocking the road by police to prevent them from holding a demonstration in front of the Council of Ministers.

On 1 May 2011, a number of security forces of the Red Mountain camp (assumed soldiers) declared a rebellion against the camp commander, because of his mistreatment of them. Demonstrators damaged camp property, including the commander's room. The location is coded as Nasr City (Geo Precision 2), which is the nearest to Jabal Al-Ahmar.

Thousands of protesters continued to demonstrate in Qina against the appointment of a former police officer   Coptic Christian as governor. Railway tracks continued to be blocked by the protesters who are allegedly headed by Salafists.

On 13 May 2011, dozens of supporters of ousted President Hosni Mubarak, gathered in front of Mustafa Mahmoud Mosque in Mohandiseen, demanding that his trial stop. They also assaulted a citizen in Mustafa Mahmoud Square, while he was driving his car and passing through the square.

On 6 May 2011, thousands of Salafists performed the absent prayer for the spirit of Osama bin Laden, the leader of al-Qaeda, at Al-Nour Mosque in Al Abbasiyah. Several of them staged a march towards the US embassy amid intense security presence. The mosque witnessed clashes between the security forces and a number of Salafists because of their insistence on performing the prayer. Other clashes and verbal exchanges occurred as well, because of the refusal of a number of worshipers to perform that prayer.

On 12 June 2011, hundreds of class teachers from different governorates staged a demonstration in front of the Organization and Administration building in Nasr City, to demand their fixation. They also broke into the main gate of the building, and prevented Safwat al-Nahas, the head of the center, from leaving, which forced him to flee the demonstrators from a back door at the center.

On 2 June 2011, dozens of assistant teachers demonstrated inside the headquarters of the National Center for Exams and Educational Evaluation in Muqattam, in demonstration against the withholding of the test result of their staff, because of errors in their data on the answer sheets, and its incompatibility with the ministry's database.There were confrontations between the assistant teachers, and a number of employees of the National Center for Exams and Educational Evaluation in Muqattam, after the teachers insisted on holding the staff inside the center and preventing them from leaving after the end of the official working hours.

On 15 June 2011, fishers of Idko Center closed Al Maddiya bridge near Khurshid (coded as Geo precision 2) with a garbage truck, concrete blocks, and two electricity poles and stones, to prevent the passage of cars and the train passing through the village, to demonstrate against the Ministry of Agriculture's decision to ban fishing from today for one month.

On 1 May 2011, there were verbal and physical confrontations between workers in Tahrir Square, celebrating their first anniversary after the January 25 revolution, against the families of the martyrs and a number of young people (pro 25 January), whom they were demonstrating against the establishment of a ceremony that was to be held this evening, with the blood of martyrs in this square. Which led to the cancellation of the artistic ceremony.

On 2 May 2011, a large number of workers and temporary staff of the forestation sector, imposed a strict siege on all entrances and exits of the Ministry of Agriculture and the reclamation of land, which prompted the security of the ministry barricade the gate closed from the inside. A number of demonstrators attacked the building, which led to breaking the glass of the main door of the ministry. They also attacked the staff and employees of the Ministry of Agriculture.

On 4 May 2011, the workers in the Support and Services Development project entered the Council of Ministers headquarters, climbed the walls, and broke the glass of gates and guard windows, after demonstrating in front of the Council to demand their confirmation after six years of their contract with Sharm el-Sheikh governorate.

On 25 May 2011, altercations occurred among nearly 150 temporary workers in the afforestation sector and car drivers, as they blocked Al Qasr al Ayni street to demand their fixation. Military and Police forces intervened to reopen the road.

On 22 June 2011, three thousand information center employees, broke into the main door of the Ministry of Local Development in Dokki, to demonstrate inside the ministry's campus, demanding the necessity of dismissing Mohsen Al-Nomani, Minister of Local Development.

On 1 May 2011, riot occurred between the villages of Tramsa and Hamedat in Qena governorate, where more than 700 people blocked the bridge between Dandara and Qena, in Tramsa, and set fire to cars and the electrical transformer, which led to power cut in the area.

On 3 May 2011, around 150 supporters of the mayor of Sharabiya and Zawiya, organized a demonstration outside the headquarters of the Ministry of Justice, which led to confrontations with a number of citizens who were in front of the headquarters of the ministry.

On 3 May 2011, demonstrators from the families of the victims of the January 25 revolution gathered in front of a court in south Cairo. After the first session of the trial of 13 police officers accused of killing and wounding demonstrators during the January 25 revolution, the court Hall witnessed a state of great anger. The victims' families destroyed the entire hall, broke the windows and the court platform and tried to break into the president's room. Before the start of the session, dozens of the people of the revolution's martyrs organized a demonstration and blocked the road in front of the South Cairo court, disrupting the traffic. Following the departure of the released defendants from the hall, demonstrators clashed with the Central Security Forces and the armed forces in an attempt to break down the barriers and capture the accused, resulting in unknown number of injuries.

On 4 May 2011, hundreds of parents of the Martyr Jawad Hosny Primary School students in Imbabah demonstrated against the transfer of the school from government to experimental status, outside the main gate of the school. The demonstrators launched an 'attack' (details not provided) on the province building, and asked to keep the school as 'governmental' and not to convert it to 'experimental', owing to the unmanageable costs they would incur.

On 6 June 2011, hundreds of Al Salam camp residents demonstrated in front of the Radio and TV Building in As Sabtiyyah, blocking the road. There were several confrontations between them and the drivers of the cars, which led the Nile Corniche to be completely paralyzed by traffic.

On 6 June 2011, dozens of families of Egyptian citizens detained in Libya, tried to enter the Foreign Ministry building by force to demand fast intervention and communication with the Libyan authorities to release their relatives, accusing the ministry of being slow to take the necessary measures to release them.

On 23 June 2011, passengers of the developed train No. 141 sat on the rails, blocking the movement of trains (Aswan - Cairo), putting wooden pieces in Raqqa station near Al Ayyat (coded as Geo precision 2) to prevent the movement of trains coming to Cairo in order to prevent the 999 air-conditioned train from crossing their train.
On 28 June 2011, there were physical confrontations between demonstrators in front of the Council of Ministers and security forces. Residents of Al Salam camps denied involvement in these clashes.
On 30 June 2011, the central security forces fired shots in the air on Ibrahim Najib Street in Cairo, which leads to the Interior Ministry, after the demonstrators threw stones at them.

Thousands of protesters continued to demonstrate in Qina against the appointment of a former police officer   Coptic Christian as governor. Railway tracks continued to be blocked by the protesters who are allegedly headed by Salafists.

Protesters continued to demonstrate in Qina against the appointment of a former police officer   Coptic Christian as governor. Railway tracks continued to be blocked by the protesters who are allegedly headed by Salafists.

About 400 Mubarak supporters protested peacefully in front of the state TV and radio building against plans to put him on trial and to denounce perceived insults directed towards the former Egyptian leader. Later, protesters demanding Mubarak stand trial marched on the state TV and radio building and clashed with the pro-Mubarak supporters. Both sides hurled stones at each other resulting in at least 3 injuries on either side.

On 14 October 2011, a number of worshipers at Al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, attacked dozens of activists and youths, who were organizing a march in front of the mosque after Friday prayers after chanting against the army and the military council, with stones.

On 10 October 2011, a group of demonstrators threw stones at the Journalists Syndicate in Qasr Al-Nile, Cairo, and assaulted the union's security, after a number of Copts were reportedly hiding inside, causing injuries, and setting fire to all signs in front of the union.

actor2: Police Forces of Egypt (2011-2012) Prison Guards
notes: On 25 October 2011, families of the detained prisoners took advantage of the strike by police secretaries, and broke into the prison in 10th of Ramadan City in Sharkia governorate, setting fire in the first floor, and a number of rioters outside and the families of the detained prisoners threw a number of Molotov cocktails, and set fire to the building from the outside, killing 1 prisoner and wounding several others including police officers. Total fatalities coded to 1.

On 10 October 2011, Ramses street witnessed fierce confrontations between Copts and Muslims, which resulted in a number of injuries, while unknown persons set fire to one of the buildings a few meters from Maydan Ramsis, Cairo, and set fire to more than seven cars. The police broke up the confrontations and fired tear gas to disperse the rioters.

On 23 October 2011, confrontations and fights took place between the citizens in the village of Bisat, Talkha Center, Dakahlia, to control the Tawhid Mosque, and between the son of the mosque's land donor and some Salafists and Ansar Al Sunna, who were accused by citizens of breaking the external door and windows of the mosque.

On 3 October 2011, dozens of technical students staged a demonstration in front of the Ministry of Higher Education headquarters in Cairo, to demand a reduction in the minimum of university admissions and open the door for transfers from one college to another. Demonstrators tried to enter the building, but the building's security stopped them, and they throw stones as well.

On 31 October 2011, Al-Azhar University students continued their sit-in for the eighth day in a row in Nasr City, Cairo, and detained the security personnel of the university building who managed to get out later. They were demanding the election of leaders, changing the student list, and amending law 103.

On 5 October 2011, students and graduates of the Tanta Nursing Institute held a demonstration and tried to enter the Ministry of Health in Cairo, for the fourth time, against the failure to respond to their demands to issue a decision to enroll them in the nursing institutes of universities.

On 31 October 2011, car drivers, motorcyclists and pedestrians clashed with a number of demonstrators, including taxi drivers from Behera, and temporary workers at the Tameya Central Hospital in Fayoum, after the demonstrators intercepted the pedestrians' road in Al Qasr al Ayni Street, Cairo, and prevented them from passing. Demonstrators also assaulted motorists, smashed a windows of a car, injured a motorcycle driver with a severe nose bleed.

On 2 October 2011, hundreds of Al-Azhar teachers blocked Salah Salem Road, besieged the headquarters of Al-Azhar sheikhs in El-Gamaleya, Cairo, attempting to enter it, while the central security forces confronted them. Teachers were demanding Al-Azhar Sheikh to immediately fixate all Al-Azhar contractors.

On 9 October 2011, hundreds of demonstrators gathered in Abdul Munim Riad Square, while stone-throwing continued between demonstrators and security forces, who fired tear gas to disperse the demonstrators.

On 9 October 2011, armed masked men continued to block the Al Arish-Al-Awja road in central Sinai near Al-Raysan village (coded as Geo precision 2), and threatened all those who tried to pass through it, demonstrating against ignoring their demands to cancel the absentee verdicts imposed on a large number of Sinai people, which are fabricated according to them.

On 28 October 2011, dozens of citizens blocked the road in front of the entrance of Sheikh Zayed City in Giza, carrying a number of banners demanding retribution for the student who was killed after policemen shot him in the car in Sheikh Zayed City, they also set fire to the police car in front of Sheikh Zayed Hospital.

On 10 October 2011, armed masked men continued to block the Al Arish-Al-Awja road in central Sinai near Al-Raysan village (coded as Geo precision 2), and threatened all those who tried to pass through it, demonstrating against ignoring their demands to cancel the absentee verdicts imposed on a large number of Sinai people, which are fabricated according to them.

On 29 April 2011, Mubarak supporters demonstrated in front of the state TV and radio building against putting him on trial and to denounce perceived insults directed towards the former Egyptian leader. Later, demonstrators demanding that Mubarak stand trial marched from Tahrir Square to the state TV and radio building and clashed with the pro-Mubarak supporters using empty bottles and stones. Clashes resulted in several injuries. Military forces intervened to disperse the demonstrators and end the clashes.

Bedouin protesters blocked roads in different places on the Sinai Peninsula to demand the release of jailed tribesmen and the revokation of prison sentences against others. They forced tour buses to stop along roads headed to Sharm El-Sheikh and Mt. Sinai.

Bedouin protesters blocked roads in different places on the Sinai Peninsula to demand the release of jailed tribesmen and the revokation of prison sentences against others. They forced tour buses to stop along roads headed to Sharm El-Sheikh and Mt. Sinai.

Pro-Mubarak supporters gather outside a television station to denounce charges laid against him and to mark his birthday. Later as anti-Mubarak supporters arrived clashes broke out with stones being thrown and traffic blocked. The army later stepped in to separate the two groups. 67 were reported injured in the violence by the Ministry of Health.
A wave of demonstrations in Manqabad over recent weeks left dozens injured during clashes with security forces. The residents are protesting against shortages of drinking water.

Thousands of demonstrators allegedly tried to storm the Israeli embassy (some demonstrators deny this) in Giza but were repulsed by military and police personnel. At least 24 stone-throwing demonstrators were injured in the incident, during which security forces fired rubbery bullets, live ammunition and tear gas at the protesters. An unspecified number of security personnel were also injured during the clashes. 350 were reported injured (45 seriously) and 140 arrested.
After a group of hardline Muslims arrived to protest the reopening of a church, Copts and Muslims threw stones at each other in the Ein Shams neighbourhood and had to be separated by military police.

Protesters outside the trial of former Interior Minister Habib el-Adly and six of his assistants broke through a cordon of riot police and attempted to storm the courthouse but were pushed back after mild clashes.

Police at the Al-Atareen police station foiled an attempt by about 100 people to set free a relative who had been detained at the station. Police were supported by local residents in their efforts to fend off the assailants.
Protesters, angry over the death of a bus driver, clash with police who respond with tear gas. This event occurred outside a police station. 10 injured.

Nearly 1,000 residents of el-Ayat staged a sit-in which disrupted rail service between Cairo and Upper Egypt to demonstrate against a mobile rely station. They were concerned over potential radiation emitted by the station. Some of the protesters were armed with shotguns which they fired into the air.
Protesters engaged in a sit-in outside the Maspero building to demand housing clashed with passersby in the area. It was not clear what prompted the violence.
Protesters threw stones at police cars escorting a former minister to his court hearing, clash with riot police. They were angry over the deferral of his trial.

About 3000 relatives of those killed during the Mubarak protests demonstrated in Tahrir Square. They clash with police who attempt to disperse them. Over 66 injured. Protests and clashes last two days. (Some reports claimed as many as 600-1,000 injured)
Protesters clash with police outside the Interior Ministry Building (El-Sayeda).
Protesters again demonstrated in Tahrir Square, resulting in new clashes with police. Many injuries reported. The protesters also allegedly have pitched tents in the Square.

Members of the April 6 Youth Movement allegedly assaulted Muslim Brotherhood members engaged in a rally outside the al-Qaid Ibrahim Mosque in Alexandria. The MB members dispersed to prevent the outbreak of clashes, although the April 6 Youth members allegedly also attacked the transports being used to ferry MB members to and from the venue. A number of injuries were also reported due to stone throwing at both MB members and passersby.

3 people were killed during riots in Salloum which broke out after the killing by police of an alleged smuggler on the border with Libya. Four police cars were torched during clashes between protesters and police/military personnel.

Hundreds of Egyptian protesters have lobbed rocks at the security headquarters and set fire to police cars for two days (Jun.5-6) in Suez, as growing impatience over delays in trying former regime officials and police accused of killing protesters. One protester was reported injured, shot in the head.
Clashes broke out between security forces and protesters calling for an end of the military governance. Many arrests are made.
Protesters clashed with military police during demonstrations against the SCAF in Alexandria.
Canal workers, upset about pay, clash with military police. 38 injuries.
Clashes in Sharm el-Sheikh resulted in 3 people injured after Mubarak was transferred from a local hospital to a courthouse in Cairo.

53 people were injured in clashes between pro- and anti-Mubarak protesters outside the Cairo police academy where his trial is being held. Police forces later separated the two sides, although stone-throwing continued.
Petrol bombs were thrown by unidentified people at the Arab League headquarters. No casualties or serious damage was reported.

34 people were injured in clashes between pro- and anti-Mubarak demonstrators outside the courthouse at the police academy where his trial is set to begin. Police reinforcements were deployed to the area after the police failed to stop the clashes.
Muslim residents of Merinab in Aswan blocked roads into the village to protest against work being done to renovate a church sometime after it began on Aug.26 (reported on Sept.16).
A large number of people protest for government reform. Injuries after fights begin with the police and military.
A large number of people protest for government reform. Injuries after fights begin with the police and military.
A large number of people protest for government reform. Injuries after fights begin with the police and military.

Demonstrations continued outside the Israeli embassy in Cairo. Demonstrators clashed with the police and threw Molotov cocktails and stones at them to which the police responded with the firing of live ammunition and tear gas. Protestors set fire to a number of vehicles that were parked in the area. The military brought reinforcements to the area. 1049 protesters were injured and 3 were killed (fatalities spread between two days of demonstrations), one of whom suffered a heart attack and died later in the hospital.
Protesters blocked Al-Arish road and marched along it to 'reclaim the revolution'. They set tires on fire and put up barricades to stop cars.
On 30 September 2011, a mob burnt down the Mar Girgis church in Marinab village in the city of Edfu, in Aswan.
Tahrir Square marched to the Israeli embassy and demolished a wall around the embassy in the Giza area of Cairo.
Coptic Christians riot, protesting against the Aswan Governor after their church is burned by a Muslim group, they believe. They continue the protest in the evening with a sit-in.

At least 36 people were killed and 272 injured in clashes between Coptic Christians and Egyptian security forces on Sunday near the state television building, known as Maspero. One soldier is killed and 20 injured. Dozens arrested.

Muslims and Christians clashed outside a hospital where protesters injured during violence in downtown Cairo were taken. A number of protesters had marched to the hospital which set off the clashes, and several cars were set on fire during the incident.

source: Committee to Protect Journalists; Daily News (Egypt); Globe and Mail (Canada); HRW; Nile News TV
notes: At least 25 were killed, including three soldiers and a journalist, and as many as 330 injured when a Coptic Christian march to the Maspero (state TV) building was opposed by the security forces. There were also claims that there were clashes between Muslims and Christians, while other Muslims were also present to protest with the Christians and were calling for unity. The army claimed some of the protesters were carrying firearms at the time, as some of the soldiers killed and wounded were shot, while others threw stones and petrol bombs. Many of those killed were allegedly run over by military APCs.

At least 10 people were injured, some by gunshots, in clashes between protesting residents and armed security guarding a building owned by a businessman. The protesters were angry over the construction of a wall in the area.

Protesters clashed with soldiers and police in Cairo after a funeral procession for a man a rights group claims died from police torture after he was caught smuggling a mobile phone chip into his cell. Dozens of protesters hurled stones at soldiers and police after relatives of Essam Atta brought his coffined body to Tahrir Square. No report of injuries or causalities.
On 6 November 2011, rioters set a fire on a railway near Sohag to denounce the nation-wide shortage of fuel.

On 17 November 2011, twenty-nine people were injured in a Cairo suburb when residents clashed with a group of Christians marching through the capital to commemorate those who died in confrontations with the army on October 9th.

On 18 November 2011, more than 2,000 Islamist and secular protesters, including April 6th Youth Movement and Al-Nour Party protesters, gathered in Cairos Tahrir Square on Friday for a mass rally to press the ruling military to hand over power to a civilian government. Police clashed with protesters when they attempt to disperse them. Some demonstrators flew the Egyptian flag, while others including ultraconservative Salafis waved a banner declaring Islam's holy book, the Quran, to be 'our constitution.' and demanded the withdrawal of the 'peace document'.

On 19 November 2011, Islamic groups convene on Tahrir Square to protest to military junta, and to demand a civilians government through elections. Riot police clear the square by using tear gas, protesters respond by throwing rocks while groups of pro-government thugs clashed with the demonstrators. 4 were reported dead while at least 750 were injured. A young man wounded on the day later succumbed to his wounds on the 30th of the month. Riot police later withdrew from the square. At least 1,500 protesters stayed in the square overnight.

On 20 November 2011, an Egyptian demonstrator was killed and 15 were wounded in protests against the country's military rulers in front of the security headquarters in the Smouha area of Alexandria. Clashes were reported between the protesters and security forces. Several thousand were reported to have taken part in the demonstrations.

On 21 November 2011, demonstrations and clashes with security forces continue into the third day. 23 were reported killed while hundreds more have been injured (some estimates were as high as 1,750). Nearby buildings were set on fire. The military governing cabinet tenders their resignations in response to the violence, though most military officials refuse. 3 Americans students were arrested for allegedly throwing petrol bombs during the protests on Sunday.

There were reports of clashes between protesters and security forces in near the security directorate office in the Smouha area of Alexandria. The clashes escalated from those reported on Monday, and left as many as 39 injured after rocks, rubber bullets and tear gas were traded between the two sides.

On 22 November 2011, the death toll from confrontations between rioters, including students and Syrians who marched in solidarity, and police around Egypt has reached 30 (26 coded in previous events), the health ministry said on Tuesday, as the violence raged into a fourth straight day, including in among tens of thousands of protesters and large numbers of police in Tahrir Square and the nearby American University. Fatalities for events on the 22nd coded to 4.

Clashes have broken out near the security directorate office in the Smouha area of Alexandria every night between Nov.19-22. 2 protesters were killd over the course of the protests (1 coded in a previous event).
Hundreds have been injured in protests and clashes with police across Egypt, including in Ismailia.
Hundreds of people marched from Al-Qaed Ibhrahim Mosque to the Northern District military leadership HQ following days of clashes with security forces in Alexandria.

Protester and police clash for the 6th straight day. Police fire teargas and rubber bullets at the demonstrators. Many report the use of live ammunition by police. The fatality count from the 6 days of protests has reached 41 (38 coded in previous events). As many as 180 police and a number of soldiers have been reported injured in the clashes.
Protesters blocked the entrance to the Egyptian cabinet HQ in a bid to prevent the newly-named PM Kamal al-Ganzuri from entering the building.

assoc_actor_1: CYR: Coalition of the Youth of the Revolution
notes: On 26 November 2011, Tahrir Square protests continued. Protesters, including the Coalition of the Youth of the Revolution vowed to remain until civilian rule is brought to the country. Medical officials said that one demonstrator has been killed outside the country's cabinet building while clashing with police.
Two anti-junta protesters described as 'rioters' by Nile News TV were arrested by police during an event near a ballot station in Nasr City.

On 29 November 2011, despite elections the previous day, between 62-80 rioters were injured in clashes with police in and around Tahrir Square involving thrown rocks, glass bottles, birdshot and Molotov cocktails. Some of those rioting also clashed with street vendors in and around Tahrir Square.

On 4 December 2011, 4 people were injured in a brawl between street vendors in Tahrir Square as they fought over the 'best spots' for sales in the area. 7 vendors wielding bladed weapons attacked the others in the Square.

assoc_actor_2: DPP: Democratic People's Party; Al-Nour Party
notes: On 15 December 2011, Muslim Brotherhood members scuffled with supporters of the DPP and al-Nour parties in the Ossim village area of Giza as the 2nd-round of polling wrapped up. Security forces later intervened to prevent further scuffles.

assoc_actor_2: CYR: Coalition of the Youth of the Revolution
notes: Scuffles were reported between Revolutionary Youth Council and FJP campaigners near the Ossims El-Bahwashi School in Giza.
Supporters of the Egyptian Bloc said their HQ was attacked by thugs who threw molotov cocktails at it.

On 16 December 2011, At least 300 are injured and 17 reported killed during clashes between military, police forces and anti-government protesters. When security forces attempted to disperse them the protests threw stones and set fire to the nearby Shura Council building. The security forces were dispersing a sit-in outside the Egyptian Cabinet building in the Tahrir Square area with several 2 army soldiers reported wounded by rioters.

On 18 December 2011, clashes between Egyptian military and police and demonstrators in Tahrir Square entered the third day, with 4 fatalities reported. Several nearby schools were closed by the Education Ministry in response to the clashes.
On 23 December 2011, police and rioters again clashed in Tahrir Square over military rule and other unpopular government decisions. More than 10,000 were gathered.
Renewed clashes inter their second day. Three reported killed.
Clashes between democracy demonstrators and members of the Muslim Brotherhood Party erupt during a larger protest. No reports of casualties.

Scores of opponents of Syrian President Bashar al-Assads regime stormed the Syrian embassy in Cairo on Friday before being dragged away by security forces. Many of the rioters were able to fight their way into the building before arrest.
Armed men scuffle with Maspero television protesters.

Thousands of protesters marched on Egypts parliament demanding a swift end to army rule, and some turned their anger on the leading Islamist movement they accused of doing the militaries bidding. Some youth members of the Muslim Brotherhood, whose Freedom and Justice Party (FJP) won nearly half the seats in the recent parliamentary election, formed a cordon in the street leading to parliament to prevent protesters from approaching the building. 'The people want the fall of the Brotherhood,' protesters chanted.

On 1 February 2012, a massive riot occurred at Port Said Stadium in Port Said, Egypt, following an Egyptian Premier League football match between El Masry and El Ahly. 74 people were killed and more than 500 were injured after thousands of El Masry spectators stormed the stadium stands and the pitch. El Ahly ultras claim that they were specifically targeted given their vocal highly televised calls for the SCAF to step down, as well as their open mockery of the previous regime and the SCAF. The ultras were one of the largest organized bodies of resistance in street protests after the absence of the Muslim Brotherhood following parliamentary elections
Ultras Protesters gather to express their anger at police not doing more to quell rioters after a recent soccer riot in Port Said where 70  people died. 388 are reported injured.
Police fire tear gas at stone throwing rioters near Tahrir Square.

In an effort to quell rioters following several days of clashes police erect a barrier near the Interior Ministry Building to insulate it from demonstrators. Despite their efforts many in the mob continued to throw rocks and petrol bombs. One reported dead of a gunshot wound, one later died due to wounds sustained.
Police fire tear gas at demonstrators who have gathered in the area and clashed with police for several days.
Hundreds of Egyptians, mainly from conservative political parties or groups, gathered in front of the Syrian Embassy in Cairo calling for the immediate discharge of the Syrian ambassador from Egypt.

Army forces clashed Tuesday with protesters and families of martyrs attending the trial of police officers accused of killing Jan. 25 protesters in Alexandria. Scuffles broke out when protesters chanted against police and army forces, which provoked the army forces.
Clashes at the Egypt/Libya border after rioters burned a military building.

Assailants attacked demonstrators gathered outside the Defense Ministry in Egypts capital to call for an end to military rule with rocks and firebombs, killing one protester and wounding 30. The protesters responded in kind. It is believed the assailants were residents of the neighborhood upset with the Salafists politics.

On 2 May 2012, unknown assailants attacked demonstrators camped outside the Defense Ministry building in Cairo (Cairo, Cairo). The demonstrators responded in kind. 9 were killed, plus at least 50 injuries. Army deployed to quell violence.
A photographer sustained an upper jaw fracture and a split upper lip when she was hit full in the face by a stone thrown by demonstrators

Egyptian troops blasted protesters with water cannons, tear gas and live ammunition, trying to prevent them from marching on the Defense Ministry in clashes that left one soldier and two journalists dead and around 373 people injured [CNN Wire]. Egypts military rulers on Saturday imposed an overnight curfew and deployed soldiers around the Defence Ministry (Reuters).
Twenty people were injured and four shops were wrecked in clashes between supporters of presidential candidates Mohamed Mursi and Ahmed Shafiq in Sohag

Unknown individuals set fire to the belongings of another group of protesters by the Supreme Court in July 26 street. The Supreme Court building witnessed some damage and tension earlier on Wednesday as raged protesters broke its interfaces and accused its staff, as they entered the building, of corruption.

A mob of hundreds of men assaulted women holding a march demanding an end to sexual harassment Friday, with the attackers overwhelming the male guardians and groping and molesting several of the female marchers.

Clashes broke out Saturday between Muslim and Coptic students in the Assiut University women s dorms after a Coptic student reportedly proselytized Christianity to Muslim students, leaving 12 students and supervisors injured.
clashes erupted after a quarrel between a lawyer and a non-commissioned police officer at Nasr City police station. Lawyers and policemen were injured
Abu Al-Ezz Al-Hareery was assaulted as he was passing by Tahrir square by a group of Muslim brotherhood protestors.

Fourteen soldiers were injured on Wednesday 18/7/2012 in clashes with protestors trying to storm the Syrian embassy in Cairo. Two armoured personnel carriers were damaged and five people were arrested.
One person died and three others were injured in clashes between security officers and protesters

Egypts health ministry has reported over 200 people have been injured and activists have said at least 37 arrested in the clashes in downtown Cairo near the US Embassy that entered their second day on Friday. At least three protesters were killed around the region.
99 policemen people have been injured in the clashes outside the US embassy between security forces and protestors at a US-movie
Clashes spread to Abd-al-Munim Riyad Square. 19 injured
Correspondent in Egypt France 24 Sonia Dridi was assaulted and suffered sexual touching to Friday in Tahrir Square in Cairo.
Family of missing boy clash with local police outside a hospital in Belqas. One person injured.

Several hundred Salafist Muslims demonstrated to demand a stronger reference to Islamic law or sharia in Egypts new constitution. The Salafis attacked the Liberals after hearing them chant slogans against Islamists and President Mursi.
As a response to the Aswan shootings, three homes and a shop, all belonging to the attackers family, were set on fire.

source: Africa News; Egyptian Organization for Human Rights
notes: Clashes started between demonstrators and police troops. The military police troops released their dogs to attack the civil demonstrators.
Protesters in Egypts capital clashed with police during a protest. 3 police injured, 3 protesters injured. Daily News Egypt puts injuries between 20 and 300
Army officers clash with policemen in New Cairo. There are unconfirmed reports that several policemen were injured, police vehicles damaged and a military officer injured by gunshot.

source: Aswat Masriya (Egypt); Bikya Masr; Daily News (Egypt)
notes: Clashes between protesters and security forces continued in Mohamed Mahmoud Street near Cairos Tahrir Square. Mubashir Misr office in downtown Cairo was burned out during violent clashes. Egypts Health Ministry has announced that the number of injuries has reached 61. Activists also said another person had been killed in the violence.
Cairos security chief and the Egyptian central security commander were assaulted in Tahrir on Wednesday during their attempt to negotiate with protesters
Demonstrators set fire to several offices belonging to the Muslim Brotherhood in reaction to President Mohammed Mursis constitutional declaration of power.
In a suburb of Cairo, an arson attack targeted an FJP (Muslim Brotherhood) office in protest at presidents new powers
Demonstrators set fire to several offices belonging to the Muslim Brotherhood in reaction to President Mohammed Mursis constitutional declaration of power

Demonstrators set fire to several offices belonging to the Muslim Brotherhood in reaction to President Mohammed Mursis constitutional declaration of power. As many as 27 injured according to Xinhua General News Service
Clashes between protesters and security forces in Cairo
At least two people were injured when pro and anti-Mursi supporters clashed. As many as 30 injured according to Xinhua General News Service
Offices of the Brotherhoods political arm were burned in Ismailia

Security forces fired tear gas at thousands of pro-democracy protesters clashing with riot police in Cairo. A residential building and a police vehicle also were burned. Three officers, including a major general, and five policemen suffered serious injuries in the area of clashes.
A young Islamist was killed on Sunday in clashes with protesters outside a local Muslim Brotherhood office in the Nile Delta. 10 people wounded.
Protesters stormed and ransacked the groups headquarters in the Nile Delta city of Mansura.
Protests erupted as supporters and opponents of Morsi clashed whilst trying to attack the police station
On 27 November 2012, people demonstrating against Mohamed Morsi clashed with the police in Cairo (Cairo, Cairo). Reportedly, the police used tear gas and one man died after inhaling the gas.
Clashes broke out in Alexandria between pro- and anti-Morsi demonstrators, but there were no immediate reports of injuries
Clashes between riot police and protesters outside the presidential palace in Heliopolis
Protesters set fire to the offices of the Muslim Brotherhood in the Mediterranean port city of Ismailiya
Protesters set fireto the offices of the Muslim Brotherhood in Suez. 8 Injured
Egypts Muslim Brotherhood supporters attacked a field hospital set up in the Roxy area of Heliopolis that was treating wounded protesters
Rival demonstrations outside the Brotherhoods headquarters in the Cairo suburb of Moqatam. MB office ransacked, police clash with anti-Morsi demonstrators.
Two journalists with Turkeys TRT television, were attacked in Tahrir Square and their equipment was damaged.

Several thousand protesters broke through a barbed-wire perimeter protecting the Cairo palace of President Mohamed Morsi to stage a noisy but peaceful demonstration against his expanded powers and proposed changes to the constitution.
Clashes between pro- and anti-Morsis decisions outside Al-Qaid Ibrahim Mosque in Alexandria
Clashes erupted between supporters and opponents of President Mohamed Mursi in Beheira. 20 people wounded
Protesters clashed with security forces and members of the Muslim Brotherhood in the Nile Delta province of Sharqiya on Friday. 25 injured
Egyptian police fired tear gas as Islamists clashed with opposition members besieging a mosque in the Mediterranean city of Alexandria. 15 injured
Unidentified men attempted to break into al-Wafd Party headquarters in Suez after smashing the buildings main entrance
Six people were injured on Friday in clashes that erupted between Islamist protesters and their opponents at Alexandrias al-Qaed Ibrahim Mosque
Seven citizens were arrested in Al-Zarqa and Faskour during the referendum and charged with rioting
Fifty five were injured on Wednesday in clashes between Ultras al-Masry Club 'hardcore football enthusiasts' and students at Port Said University hostel
Egypts Interior Ministry said that investigations have revealed that a scuffle between a now-arrested defendants and a protester started an attack that led to more than 15 injuries on Saturday.

Police in Egypt clashed on Friday with a crowd of furious Muslims who tried to storm a church after claiming a Coptic man was involved in an case of child abuse. Dozens of Muslims went on the rampage in the southern city of Qena, destroying two Coptic-owned shops and then marching on the church in a bid to storm it before police fired tear gas to disperse them.

Scuffles resumed on Sunday in Alexandria between the security forces and the protesters as the latter torched the third police car after setting fire to two central security cars. Protesters broke into the courthouse and started a small fire, which was soon extinguished.

Clashes broke out on Tuesday in front of the police station of Matrouh Governorate.The clashes took place between the families of people accused in a smuggling case and the security forces as they tried to break in the police station. The security fired bullets and teargas bombs to disperse the people. 46 injured (24 of whom policemen).

Four people on Thursday were injured in clashes between protesters and security forces in Cairos al-Qasr al-Aini street and Tahrir Square. Police clashed with protesters who tried to dismantle a wall of concrete blocks shutting off a street leading to Cairos Tahrir Square, to ease the movement of demonstrators. Egyptian protestors set fire to an attachment of the Scientific complex in downtown Cairo on Thursday, following skirmishes between protestors and security men.
Egyptian protestors set fire to an attachment of the Scientific complex in downtown Cairo on Thursday, following skirmishes between protestors and security men.
Egyptian protesters on Friday set fire to the Muslim Brotherhood party headquarters in the canal city of Ismailiya.
In Ismailiya, protesters entered the building, set fire to a room used by security services and looted furniture and equipment.

Five people were killed on Friday during clashes between police and protesters in the canal city of Suez. A number of protesters also broke into the Suez city council and declared a sit-in amid ongoing clashes with security forces surrounding the building. Over 30 injured in clashes.
Scores of protesters on Friday cut off the Corniche and the tramway in al-Qaed Ibrahim Mosques area in Alexandria and placed cement barriers on the tramway.
The Muslim Brotherhoods website, Ikhwan Online, reported on Friday that unidentified assailants attempted to torch its headquarters in Tawfiqiya in downtown Cairo. Several injuries reported.

Dozens of protesters on Friday hurled stones at security forces stationed behind the cement wall in Sheikh Rihans Street in downtown Cairo. TV footage on al-Arabiya news channel showed police forces hurling teargas canisters at the protesters.

Clashes erupted on Friday noon between protesters and security forces in front of the local municipality in Alexandrias Kom al-Dekka neighbourhood. An Aswat Masriya eyewitness reported the absence of security bodies in most areas where protests are taking place in the city. 19 injured.
A number of protesters on Friday broke into the Suez city council and declared a sit-in amid ongoing clashes with security forces surrounding the building. Over 30 injured in clashes.

An Egyptian group called 'Black Block' on 25 January attacked a police station in the north coastal city of Alexandria. A journalist said the group, whose members wore black uniform and were all masked, attacked Al-Manshiyah police station and threw Molotov Cocktails at the police forces there. He said the police forces responded by opening fire in the air. They also tried to attack the municipal council building in the city, but the security forces managed to prevent them and cordoned off the building.

Clashes broke out between police and protesters on Saturday in the Egyptian canal city of Suez amid deadly unrest sparked by death sentences handed down to 21 people over football-related violence last year.
Clashes erupted on Saturday between security forces and protesters following the verdict announced earlier by Port Said Criminal Court resulting in at least 30 fatalities.

In Tanta, hundreds of protesters attempted to break into Tanta courts complex on Saturday but were stopped by security forces. The situation intensified when protesters started hurling rocks at security forces, which responded by firing tear gas into the crowds of protesters.
300 people have gathered in Nubar Street, hurling stones at the security forces safeguarding the Interior Ministry
Rioting sparked by death sentences passed on fans of a local football team rocked Egypts Port Said for a second straight day on Sunday, leaving another 7 people dead and more than 460 injured

Hundreds of demonstrators began on Sunday night to block entrances and exits of most bridges in Cairo in protest against the speech President Mohamed Mursi gave earlier. Protesters torched car tires and used them to block the entrances and exits.

Television footage showed on Sunday heavy clashes between protesters and security forces near Cairos Nile Corniche. Teargas was fired by security forces heavily on protesters, while the protesters threw stones at them. The nearby French Lycee building was also gutted by a fire started overnight during clashes between protesters and security forces.
An office belonging to the FJP party was set aflame in Damanhour city of Beheira . A real estate taxes building in the same area was robbed before also being burnt.
In Cairo the French Lycee building was gutted by a fire started overnight during clashes between protesters and security forces near Tahrir Square.
Gunfire and tear gas were used as clashes continued in Port Said on Sunday, raising the total number of casualties to at least 34 dead and 416 injured; 17 suffered gunshot wounds.
Two people died in fighting between protesters and security forces in the riot-hit canal city of Port Said on Monday, including one in front of a police station.
Meanwhile, dozens from the Popular Current youth cut on Monday the international Blatim road which connects Kafr al-Sheikh to Baltim.
Scuffles erupted between protesters and security forces in downtown Cairo, resulting in the injury of dozens, and torching an armored vehicle that belonged to the security forces. One person killed.

Scuffles erupted between the security forces and protesters on Monday, leaving 16 of the security forces injured, as the protesters threw bricks at them causing minor head wounds. An eyewitness told Aswat Masriya that the scuffles resulted in torching an armored vehicle after the protesters threw Molotov cocktails at it. The scuffles left around 112 protesters injured and many more suffered from suffocation because of the teargas.

The governorate of Kafr El-Sheikh also witnessed renewed violent clashes outside the Governorate Building. According to Al-Shorouk News, 92 were injured including 13 police officers. Many of the injured had suffocated from the heavy use of teargas by police forces.
At least three people reportedly in possession of Molotov cocktails were arrested in Mansoura, Daqahleya. Protesters had blocked a major road and the railway during the Friday protest.

Protesters threw stones and bottles at the Egyptian prime ministers motorcade on Saturday as he tried to enter Cairos Tahrir Square after overnight clashes nearby and at the presidential palace, 11 people injured
Injuries were also reported in Kafr El-Sheikh, where according to the health ministry 13 people were confirmed injured after clashing with police.
Clashes erupted between security forces and young protestors in Al-Shaykh Rihan Street, near to the Ministry of Interior. Protestors hurled stones at security forces, which responded with tear gas.

Clashes broke out in the Nile Delta on Monday between demonstrators and riot police after the funeral of Mohammed al-Guindi, who died in custody. Protestors threw stones at police, who responded tear gas and birdshot.The chief of police said the protesters torched an armoured police vehicle and threw petrolbombs at provincial and police headquarters. He said police detained up to nine people.18 injured.
In Kafr al-Zayat, two people were injured in altercations with the police in front of a police station after the protesters had attempted to break into the station.

Scores of protesters on Friday hurled stones and petrol bombs at Malahas city council in an attempt to break into the building. Demonstrators set on fire the Freedom and Justice Party (FJP) headquarters. 28 injured in Gharbiya

Egyptian police on Friday fired tear gas at protesters who lobbed petrol bombs and set off fireworks outside the presidential palace, amid nationwide rallies against President Mohamed Morsi. Spokesman for the Ministry of Health stated one person dead and 216 injured across country.

In Al-Gharbiyah Governorate, hundreds of demonstrators hurl stones at members of Al-Mahallah City Council, attempt to storm councils offices amid strict measures by security forces. Protesters hurled rocks and Molotov cocktails at the building and security forces, who responded by firing warning shots in the air and teargas bombs. Protesters blocked Al-Bahr Street using car tires, disrupting traffic and transportation within the city. On Friday, hundreds protested against President Mohamed Morsi and the Muslim Brotherhood at Al-Bahr Street, blocking the street and preventing cars from passing through. A driver hit four protesters and ran over 14 year-old protester, Mahmoud El-Qott as he passed through the street.El-Qott was transferred to hospital, but died shortly after he arrived.

A funeral for Egyptian police officer Hisham Tema took place in Beni Sweif on Saturday. Tema was injured during his attempt to settle an armed clash between two families in el-Ghamrawi and Ezbat el-Safeeh areas. He passed away in hospital after one hour as he sustained serious injuries. (Date of death unspecified)

Security source at Interior Ministry reports arresting 57 rioters in Cairo, Al-Gharbiyah, Alexandria governorates. Source says 30 arrested outside Al-Qubbah Palace, including one in possession of gun.

Security source at Interior Ministry reports arresting 57 rioters in Cairo, Al-Gharbiyah, Alexandria governorates. Source says 30 arrested outside Al-Qubbah Palace, including one in possession of gun.

In the city of Mahalla, protesters blocked the main road near Shoan Square by burning tyres, demanding President Mohamed Morsis regime 'be put on trial for its crimes' and chanting against the policeProtesters also closed down all shops in the square. Security forces attempted to disperse the demonstrations which resulted in clashes with protesters throwing rocks at police who are launching teargas canisters from within armoured vehicles. Hundreds of protesters attacked the police station with rocks and Molotov cocktails on Friday, leaving several police officers injured.

Security forces in Daqahliya on Monday teargassed protesters who gathered around the city council building and attacked security forces. The demonstrators stopped traffic in al-Geish street near the building and hurled stones at security forces.

In Daqahleya, clashes took place at noon on Monday in front of the governorate building.16 protesters were arrested, 14 of them minors, while three journalists were attacked, according to 6 April activist Wael Ghareeb, by Muslim Brotherhood members. Police forces dispersed the clashes using teargas bombs.

A number of protesters were injured on Tuesday in Mansoura, in the governorate of Daqahleya, following violent clashes that broke out between protesters and Central Security Forces (CSF).During the clashes CSF reportedly used extensive amounts of teargas, in addition to birdshot, while protesters threw Molotov cocktails and rocks at security forces.

Kom Ombos church, Mar Girgis, has been under attack for the past three days from what residents described as 'unknown assailants.' Mostly in their teens, hundreds of young boys and men have been surrounding the church, showering it with rocks and Molotov cocktails, with Central Security Forces (CSF) trucks and soldiers fighting the assailants with teargas grenades.

Hundreds protested in Talaat Harb Square in Downtown Cairo on Saturday against President Morsi and the Muslim Brotherhood, in solidarity with the citizen killed by Central Security Forces (CSF) in Mansoura on Friday. During the protest, minor clashes broke out between protesters and street vendors, who accused protesters of paralysing their work. Arguments also took place between protesters and car drivers who were angry because the protest caused traffic jam.
Security agencies in Suhaj foil attempt to break into police department by unidentified individuals protesting dismissal of chief detective.

Protesters threw petrol bombs and stones at the police station in Port Said after the authorities decided to move prisoners awaiting a verdict over alleged involvement in a deadly football riot. Police responded with tear gas. 253 people were injured in the clashes.

Violent clashes between police forces and civilians broke out late Sunday in front of the InterContinental Semiramis Hotel on Cairos Nile Corniche. Al-Ahrams Arabic-language news portal reported that a number of rioters near Abdel-Moneim Riyad Square had set a police vehicle ablaze, before Central Security Forces replied with teargas.
Security forces stormed Cairos Tahrir Square at dawn to open it to traffic.According to the TV, 39 people were arrested with guns, Molotov cocktails and tear gas in their possession.
Elsewhere in Egypt, dozens of protesters blocked el-Bahr street at the Shoon Square in Gharbiyas Mahalla city with burning tires and roadblocks. Protesters also hurled stones at passing cars.
Meanwhile, the Niles Courniche downtown withstands chasing between the security forces and the protesters, as they hurl stones at each other for the second day in a row.

Fresh clashes broke out on Tuesday between police and protesters in the restive Suez Canal city of Port Said, Tension was running high around the main security building, which was set ablaze on Tuesday for the second day in a row, with anti-riot police firing tear gas at protesters hurling petrol bombs. One person died from head wound caused by teargas canister fired by police during protests.

Ultras Ahlawy youth raised chants against the interior ministry before clashing with the security forces in the Dokki neighborhood of Giza, The protesters and the security forces hurled stones at each other. The security forces fired teargas bombs while the protesters used fireworks.

Members of Egypts Ultras Ahlawy groups protested on Wednesday at the Giza security directorate, At least five-thousand Ultras members gathered in protest. The angry young protesters threw fireworks at the police building and set on fire an empty police truck parked outside.
Two people were also injured during clashes in Ismailia.

Clashes re-erupted Wednesday between tens of protesters and police near the US embassy where security presence is most intense. Protesters, mostly young, blocked the Nile Corniche with a tree as they continued to throw rocks at the police.

Meanwhile, in the city of Mahala Kubra in the governorate of Gharbia, an aswat masriya witness said that the governorates security directorate has sent for additional security reinforcements after the clashes escalated between the security forces and protesters.
Clashes in the governorates of Dakahlia resulted in two people injured.

A group of Damietta residents took out their anger on banners of the Freedom and Justice Party (FJP) headquarters during Mohamed El-Shafaais funeral on Tuesday. One participant stated that the procession participants smashed the FJP banners to express their anger. Tayseer Abdallah, FJP member of the Shura Council from Damietta, denied that the party headquarters was attacked.

Egyptian police fired tear gas at protesters as clashes resumed on Thursday in Port Said. The daily clashes resumed after protesters marched on the police headquarters. Police fired tear gas as soldiers, deployed in the city since late January, moved in to guard the police building. An Egyptian protester was shot dead and 73 people reported injured.
Sporadic clashes between security forces, demonstrators take place in Nile Corniche.

Clashes erupted in the Gharbeya city of Mahalla between protesters and police forces in front of the Second Mahalla Police Station, exchanging rocks and teargas canisters.Protesters surrounded the station, calling for the removal of President Morsi and Minister of Interior Mohamed Ibrahim, and the end of Muslim Brotherhood rule.

Friday saw modest protests against President Mohamed Morsi and the Ministry of Interior in the Delta and Lower Egypt regions as well as Cairo and Alexandria. Dozens of youth clashed with policemen on the nearby Qasr Al-Nil bridge and in Simon Bolivar Square near the American Embassy.
Alexandria Protesters have broken into the temporary governorate building in Alexandria. The main governorate building was abandoned following intense clashes in the area last week.

Seven people were injured in renewed violent confrontations that took place late on Saturday by a Mahala police station in Gharbiya. Trying to raid the station, protesters hurled stones and Molotov cocktails at security forces who responded with teargas to disperse them.

Dozens of protesters gathered on Sunday morning in Bir al-Abd in Northern Sinai, locking the doors to the City Council Building and preventing workers from entering while setting tires alight. They demanded that the government provide jobs for the unemployed and chanted against the Muslim Brotherhood,

source: Egypt State Information Service; Nile News TV
notes: Troops deployed near some embassies in downtown Cairo are being attacked by rioters, said the ministry. Clashes between protesters and security forces continued for the second day in a row following a court verdict in the so-called Port Said Stadium 'killing' case on 9 March

source: Egypt State Information Service; Nile News TV
notes: Minor clashes broke out at Helwan University on Sunday between students affiliated with the Muslim Brotherhood and revolutionary students a few hours prior to the student union elections beginning in the university. Eventually, confrontations broke out between the chanting students and students affiliated with the Brotherhood, according to Wessam El-Bakry, spokesperson of Soot El-Talaba.

Clashes between protesters and security forces around Mahalla Second Police Station continued on Sunday, although the stations police officers are on strike.Protesters gathered in Al-Shoon Square on Sunday before moving towards the nearby Mahalla Second Police Station, which they attacked with rocks and Molotov cocktails, according to activist and Mahalla resident Mahmoud Haroun.

At least 24 people were arrested in the aftermath of clashes with the police in front of Shebin Al-Kom court in Menufiya on Wednesday.Hundreds of Ultras Ahlawy members, Al-Ahly football club fans, surrounded Shebin Al-Kom court in Menufiya on Wednesday demanding the release of Mohamed Gamal Helal, a member of the group. Helal was arrested in front of the security directorate and was charged with vandalism and being a member of the Black Bloc group
Clashes erupted in the working class neighbourhood of Shubra on Monday night, leaving three dead and 26 injured, according to the Ministry of Health, in a street fight between two families.

Meanwhile, in Manial district of Giza, some anti-MB protesters broke into the headquarters of the groups political arm Freedom and Justice Party (FJP), according to state-run Al-Ahram Online news website. The ground floor of the FJP headquarters was totally smashed including the offices and the furniture, while some demonstrators attempted to set fire to the place until others prevented them.
28 people were injured so far in clashes between pro and anti-Muslim Brotherhood protesters in Cairos Al-Muqattam district and in Al-Sharqiyah governorate.

Around 200 Islamists blocked Gate 2 of the Egyptian Media Production City (EMPC) on Sunday in protest of what they describe as media bias against Islamists and unfair coverage of the Friday clashes in front of the Muslim Brotherhood headquarters.Several newspapers such as state-owned Al-Ahram reported that their journalists were attacked as they attempted to cover the protest.At the time of print reports from inside EMPC indicated there were clashes between protesters and security forces. A truce was negotiated shortly after.

When news broke that student Omar Yassin had received a one-month suspension for his participation in demonstrations, the demonstrating students became outraged and attempted to enter the campus, Alaa said.Security forces from an independently contracted security company then began attacking students with rubber bullets and rocks in addition to spraying them with fire extinguishers in an attempt to keep them at bay, student Haleem El-Sharani said. The crowd of students began retaliating in anger against the security forces that in turn fought back as the violence escalated late into the afternoon

Egypts state-owned Channel One ran the following urgent caption: 'Reporter of Egyptian television: Partial closure of Tahrir square for traffic.'Following there was another caption: 'Reporter of Egyptian television: Clashes between protestors and drivers of vehicles in Tahrir square.'

In an impromptu move, 6 April Youth Movement- the group led by Ahmed Maher- surrounded the house of the Minster of Interior Mohamed Ibrahim, in a protest labeled ' a prostitute of all regimes'; in reference to the ministry of interior. Hundreds of the movements members gathered at 1.30 am on Friday holding female undergarments, which they threw at the minsters house, as a sign of political prostitution and subservience to the current government.The protesters chanted against the police and demanded justice for all who were killed in clashes.At time of reporting, the movements Spokesperson Mohamed Adel reported clashes between the protesters and the police, in which teargas and bird-shots were used against 'unarmed' demonstrators, with at least two members arrested.
Clashes between demonstrators take place in Sayyidi Jabir area, Alexandria. Demonstrators hurled stones, Molotov cocktails.

In Alexandria, 12 policemen were injured as 200 people attempted to break into Al-Raml police station after an overnight fight between some Islamist supporters and liberal opponents of President Mohamed Morsi, MENA quoted General Amin Ezz Eddin, assistant interior minister and head of Alexandria police department, as saying. Protesters subsequently deny attacking police station.
35 ambulances were deployed in riotous governorates; 15 in Cairo s Tahrir Square, 10 in Alexandria and 10 in Gharbiyas city of Tanta

Meanwhile, another 10 policemen were injured at Beheiras Wady al-Natron police station as about 150 people hurled stones at the station and attempted to break into it until security men shot in the air and fired teargas bombs to disperse them.

Violent clashes broke out on Tuesday between Al-Ismaily football club fans and Central Security Forces (CSF). Several Al-Ismaily fans and CSF personnel were reportedly injured after the match at Air Defence stadium.

Anti-Mursi protesters in Alexandria clashed with unknown persons in Somouhas Victor Emmanuel Square on Friday. Central security forces tear-gassed both parties to disperse them metres away from Alexandrias security directory.

Protesters battled police in several Egyptian cities on Saturday, with at least eight people reported injured, after rallies to mark the anniversary of a key opposition movement turned violent. The assaults were allegedly undertaken by both Mubarak supporters and plainclothes security personnel, sometimes supporting each other, including rioters handing captured journalists over to the police. Equipment was also reportedly smashed. Some of the targets including Arab, Russian, US, European and South American journalists, as well as locals.
Military forces forcibly move 1,000 protesters away from pro-regime in an effort to quell violence.
A Cairo bureau of Al Jazeera is ransacked by 'thugs'.
As many as 4,000 protesters blocked a railway line and burned tires in an anti-government demonstration.

Around 3,000 protesters demanding cheaper housing set ablaze the governorate headquarters in the city east of Cairo. The governors car parked outside was also torched. The army, stationed nearby, did not intervene.

On 9 February 2011, continued clashes between security forces and demonstrators in Kharga Oasis (A-Dakhla Oasis, New Valley) resulted in 2 more killed and many more injured. Demonstrators allegedly also set a police station and courthouse on fire to demand the removal of the provincial security chief.

A group of about 1,000 protesters broke off from a larger demonstrations and attacked a police station and police vehicles with molotov cocktails. 10 people were killed and 50 injured during resulting clashes with police. Police allegedly surrendered to the protesters after the clashes and the protesters proceeded to release relatives and others held at the station.

1,000 Police officers march on the grounds of the interior ministry building to protest for higher pay. They scuffled with military forces who fired into the air to disperse them. They later withdrew to avoid antagonizing protesters.
Pro-democracy supporters clash with pro-Mubarak supporters as the former protested. 10 dead, plus many others wounded.

4 Coptic monks and 1 army officer were injured when soldiers arrived at the Bishop Bishoy Monastery in Wadi Natrun and demolished a wall the monks had built after hearing about prison breaks during recent anti-government protests.

After 4-days of protests outside the Interior Ministryto demand better salaries, police who had been dismissed from their positions used firebombs to set parts of the building on fire during continued protests. Soldiers failed to disperse the crowd despite firing into the air.

On 22 February 2011, angry villagers set fire to two Bahai homes after demonstrations against local authorities refusal to upgrade a marina for ferries connecting their island village to the mainland. The homes were owned by an official and were vacant at the time.

The military forcibly removed Friday of Cleansing and Protecting the Revolution protesters with batons as they enforced a midnight curfew. Clashes and injuries were reported. The military later apologized for the aggressive use of force.
On 25 January 2011, in North Sinai, hundreds of people rioted in Al Arish, Joura, Mahadiyah and Sheikh Zuweiyid. (coded as 4 events)
On 25 January 2011, in North Sinai, hundreds of people rioted in Al Arish, Joura, Mahadiyah and Sheikh Zuweiyid. (coded as 4 events)

On Jan 26-27, in Cairo, 3,000 people demonstrated in front of the Lawyers' Association Building. Police Forces tried to force rioters, who decided to stay overnight inside the building, to leave (around 200 people) using tear gas. Clashes were reported in Ramses Street. On Jan 27, hundreds of people were still demonstrating in front of the Lawyers' Association Building. In the evening, Police Forces managed to enter the building to disperse rioters, injuring several.
On 26 January 2011, in Baltim, some 1,000 people demonstrated against the government. They clashed with Police Forces who used tear gas against rioters. Several people were injured.

On 26 January 2011, in Suez, a group of people attended the religious funerals of a man who died during clashes with Police Forces the day before. Police Forces prevented people from marching in the streets to the cemetery, which led to renewed violent clashes.
