On 27 January 2011, in Sallum, a 'large' group of inhabitants rioted, destroying buses and throwing stones at a police station. Tensions with Police Forces were reported, though there was no reporred interaction.
On Jan 30 or Jan 31 (date unclear), in Suez, rioters attacked Ataqa Police Station and allegedly released 85 undocumented Eritreans.

On 3 February 2011, in Al Qantara Al Gharbiyya, hundreds of rioters blocked the road between Port Said and Ismailia, and threw stones at policemen. At least seven were injured, including two police officers.
Two are killed in prison riot, 10 other wounded when guard open fire.
On 9 February 2011, in Bulaq, train workers demonstrated to demand better salaries. The demonstrators gathered on the rail lines, preventing trains from entering.

Most of the Central Auditing Agencys staff staged a protest demanding the departure of the agenys chairman. A fire later broke out on the second floor of the agency building. The chairman accused the protesters of setting the fire.
On 10 February 2011, transport workers demonstrated on the train tracks of the train station in Alexandria, shutting down train transportation, to demand better working conditions.
On 10 February 2011, Abou Ghattas Parkings workers demonstrated on the train tracks, in Ramses station, Cairo, preventing the operation of the railways to demand better working conditions.
On 14 February 2011, a number of demonstrators took over the National Party's headquarters in Edfu, and put the land on sale. Lootings and destruction of goods around were also reported.

On 14 February 2011, in Cairo, tens of workers and activists demonstrated in front of the General Union of Workers' headquarters. Several people inside the building allegedly threw empty bottles at the protesters, injuring several of them.

On 14 February 2011, in Nasr City, around 2,000 employees from Al Azhar University entered Al Azhar to demand the resignation of Dr. Salah Sadiq Siddiq, the university's president. The demonstrators reportedly destroyed furniture in the offices. Demonstrators demanded better working conditions and an end to corruption.
On 14 February 2011, in Mansoura, train workers went onto the tracks and prevented the trains from entering the station in a demonstration against corruption.
On 17 February 2011, in Cairo, workers from Mahmoud Al Morshedy Company threw stones at the doors and glasses of the firm's headquarters. Police Forces intervened to disperse rioters.

On 20 February 2011, in Doqqi, after a car prevented an employee from entering the Agricultural Hospital and allegedly hit him, a number of people demonstrating clashed with security forces from the Ministry of Local Development.

On 22 February 2011, in Zahraa Al Maadi, more than 1,500 workers from a group of piping companies barricaded the road to Katameya, to demand higher wages and better working conditions. Demonstrators also burned tires.

On 22 February 2011, in Hurghada, in the morning, nearly 2,000 police officers from various cities of the Red Sea governorate marched in the streets to demand better working conditions. They barricaded a road.
On 23 February 2011, in Awsim, a number of Muslim Brotherhood activists entered and took control of the National Democratic Party's headquarters, forcing the Party's secretary to leave the building.

On 24 February 2011, following a dispute between two families in Othman Moharram street, in Al Omraneyah, police officers attempted to interfere. One policeman, assaulted by one man, shot and killed a bystander by mistake. To avoid retaliation from the young man's relatives, police forces set up a security cordon in front of Omraneyah Police Station.

On 25 February 2011, following Friday's prayers, people rallied to demand a new government and called for the dismissal of hold-overs from the regime of ousted President Hosni Mubarak. Demonstrators clashed with Police Forces in front of the Security Directorate, throwing stones at policemen. Police used tear gas and fired at the rioters to disperse them.
Egyptian soldiers attempted to break up clashes between Copts and Muslims in the Muqattam district of Cairo with aerial firing but were not able to separate the two groups of rioters.

Clashes between Copts and Muslims were reported in the Muqattam, Al Sayyida Aisha, El-Basatin, and Al Qalah areas of Cairo after Copts protested the burning of a church last week. At least 13 people (6 Copts and 7 Muslims) were killed and 140 injured (68 Copts and 72 Muslims) in the violence which broke out when Muslims attacked the Coptic protesters leading to stone throwing and clashes. Most of the casualties were reported in the Muqattam area, although at least 1 occurred around the blocked Autostrad.

After some men heckled a protest by women for more rights, the army was forced to intervene by firing into the air to disperse the rioters who attempted to attack the women. Some female protesters were beaten.

On 8 March 2011, Egyptian women and some men demonstrated in Cairo - Qasr al Nile (Kasr Al Nile, Cairo) in favor of giving women more of a voice in building Egypts future. Crowds of men turned up at the protest to assault the demonstrators and eventually chased them out of the Square after they outnumbered them. Women were reportedly beaten by the men.

Clashes between Salafists and Copts broke out on Autostrad road in the El-Basatin Sharkeya area of Cairo as Copts protested the burning of a church last week. At least one Copt was killed and 5 people injured during the fight.
13 Christians are dead after a group of Muslims clash with protesting Christians. 110 wounded. Is a battle rather than VAC, see source file p16

On 10 September 2011, hundreds of demonstrators gathered in front of the Al Jizah police headquarters. Demonstrators attempted to storm the building and threw stones at the police to which the police responded with tear gas and warning shots in the air. Tens of demonstrators were injured.
On 21 September 2011, members of the El-Fawkhriya Clan barricaded three roads in Al Arish following the arrest of the 3 members of the clan by police in the town.

Violent clashes broke out between pro- and anti-Mubarak protesters in front of the Egyptian Parliament (about 2 blocks from Tahrir Square). About 200 supporters of the former president clashed with 300 anti-government protesters, with both sides throwing bottles and stones before police intervened to break them up.

On 1 November 2011, a group of April 6th Movement supporters marched to Talaat Harb Square where they briefly scuffled with residents of the area before police broke up the fight. The group was marching against the continued detention of Alaa Abdel Fatteh, the death of other activists, and against the continued use of the emergency law. Neither injuries nor fatalities were reported.

notes: Police detained a score of men who attempted to storm El-Mazraa Prison in the Torah area of Cairo and free hundreds of inmates. Those detained said they were attempting to release their relatives and friends who they say should have been freed under a new decree from the Interior Minister.

On 18 November 2011, a number of rioters broke into a building next to the Ministry of Interior in the Abdin neighborhood of Cairo, thinking that television cameras in the building were broadcasting a story describing demonstrators in Tahrir Square as thugs. As it turned out, the cameras were only taking pictures of the demonstrators. On the same day, demonstrators marched to the Ministry of Interior building and attempted to break inside before police responded with tear gas. Demonstrators also set tires on fire to prevent police from returning to Tahrir Square.

On 21 November 2011, demonstrators attempted to storm the Security Directorate building in Al Mansurah when police responded by firing rubber bullets and live ammunition, wounding a number of demonstrators. The demonstrations turned into hit-and-run attacks between the rioters and police. No fatalities were reported.

On 21 November 2011, more than 2,000 demonstrators clashed with police in front of the Security Directorate in the town of Asyut with the former throwing bricks and stones and the latter responding with tear gas. A number of demonstrators were wounded although no number was recorded.

On 29 November 2011, a number of individuals broke into a voting location in the Halwan neighborhood of Cairo after voting had closed. Police and army soldiers removed the individuals which caused injuries. No fatalities were reported.
On 5 December 2011, rioters attacked the car of the president of the electoral counting commission and stole electoral papers as the car drove through the Al Salam district of Cairo.
On 5 December 2011, thugs attacked voters in the Qasr Al-Nile district of Cairo while accusing Muslim Brotherhood cadres of rigging the election. Neither injuries nor fatalities were reported.

A group of Islamists supporting former President Mohamed Mursi smashed several shops in Alexandrias Miami district and prevented residents in the area from celebrating Mursis ouster. According to AFP, one person killed.
Retired military personnel protest for fair pensions and health insurance at the ministry of defence

Mobs, continuing to express their displeasure with police following deadly soccer riot in Port Said clash with police for the second day. 1,482 reported injured over the two days, Twelve killed total.
Three die in bread riots over the course of a few days. The price of most goods has risen as much as 300% since unrest began.
Protesters gather in various cities to call for an end of the interim government. The protests lasted over the course of several days.

Egyptian soldiers fired into the air to disperse protesters who were allegedly trying to storm a Suez Canal authority office in Ismailia. The protesters were employees of the canal authority and had been engaged in ongoing demonstrations to demanding higher salaries and better working conditions.

Protesters in Tahrir Square blocked access to Cairos largest government office as part of ongoing protests to put pressure on the SCAF to continue reforms and prosecute corruption. About 27 protesters also began a hunger strike to demand the government address the demonstrators grievances.

Tens of thousands of people protested in Tahrir Square to show solidarity and unity in their efforts to demand reform following former President Mubaraks ouster. A number of different parties and organizations were represented, including Islamist groups.

Thousands of Egyptians marched across the country on Friday to denounce the rule of Islamist President Mohammed Morsi and his fundamentalist Muslim Brotherhood. In Alexandria, protesters tore down a Brotherhood sign at the groups office and set it on fire. 20 injured.
In Tanta security forces fired tear gas canisters in clashes with rock-throwing protesters. 65 injured.

Protesters locked shut the doors of the main administrative building for state services just outside the station at Tahrir Square, while others blocked traffic at a main bridge on the other side of town near the presidential palace by standing in the road.

Security forces sprayed protesters with water hoses and tear gas outside the presidential palace. Some protesters broke out in chants: 'The people want to bring down the regime,' while others responded by throwing stones.
About 1,000 protesters gathered in central Cairos Tahrir Square, a protest hub. 'The revolution continues' read some banners, as others chanted 'After blood has been spilled, there is no legitimacy.'
Hundreds of government opponents marched through the streets of Alexandria.
Nearly 1,000 women gathered in Cairo to denounce recent sexual assaults.

In a show of support for the president on Friday, around 5,000 Islamist supporters of the Gamaa Islameya (Islamic Group) and its political arm (the Building and Development Party) gathered in Al Nahda Square in front of Cairo University for a rally dubbed 'No to violence.'
Another march headed to the main courthouse to demand justice for a 12 year-old vendor killed by the military earlier this month.

Thousands of soccer fans and anti-government protesters blocked roads and enforced a work stoppage in Egypts restive city of Port Said to protest 'injustices' by the government, disrupting rail services and forcefully evicting workers from their factories and the provincial governments offices. The 'Green Eagles' fan club also disrupted work in the Mediterranean cities main telephone exchange and sent students home from several schools in their Sunday protest.

In the southern province of Assiut, private minibus drivers who operate local transportation lines have protested a lack of diesel for the past few days, waiting in a parking lot for hours and threatening to block railroads if diesel is not made available.

A third day of protests and strikes brought the restive city of Port Said to a halt on Tuesday, as demonstrators demanded security officials be held accountable for the killings of over 40 residents in riots last month.

Thousands of brick workers blocked railroad tracks from a southern city to Cairo for a second day Sunday to protest rising industrial oil prices, causing the cancellation of some services, security officials said.

An Egyptian security official says protesters have thrown firebombs and rocks at police who tried to re-open Cairos central Tahrir Square, where a sit-in has brought downtown traffic to a standstill for nearly three months. Around 70 protesters and street vendors were arrested during the skirmish at Tahrir. At least 13 children were among the 56 arrested when security forces raided Tahrir Square early on Tuesday morning.The report stated that the ages of the detained children ranged between 12 and 18.

Clashes erupted when dozens of residents threw firebombs and rocks at police. In anticipation of renewed clashes, police had set up checkpoints and increased their presence around the Mar Girgis church in Aswan. The clashes resumed on Friday afternoon between the police forces and protesters who accused the church of kidnapping a Muslim woman with the intention of converting her to Christianity. They also tried to storm into the church which caused the injury of three, one of them a police officer. The security forces threw teargas bombs to disperse the protesters.

Police arrested six persons who threw petrol bombs and stones at Mar Girgis church in Aswan. The clashes resumed on Friday afternoon between the police forces and protesters who accused the church of kidnapping a Muslim woman with the intention of converting her to Christianity. They also tried to storm into the church which caused the injury of three, one of them a police officer. The security forces threw teargas bombs to disperse the protesters. Major General Hassan Abdul-Hay, security chief of Aswan, told reporters late Saturday that 'family and social reasons,' not religion, were behind her disappearance, and she had not converted.

Egypts Interior Ministry, which oversees the countries police force, said a protester died and dozens were wounded before dawn Saturday in Mansoura where about 400 people protested outside the local council office. The ministry said protesters were chanting anti-government slogans before they cut off a main road and threw firebombs at the building. Activists there told The Associated Press that a protester, Hossam Eldin Abdullah Abdelazim, was killed when an armored police vehicle crushed him to death during the clashes. A funeral was held for him later in the day. One report suggests 4 people were killed, another report claims 2 others died.

source: AFP; Al Ahram; AP; Daily News (Egypt)
notes: Also Saturday, a police car in the restive Suez Canal city of Port Said when the protesters refused to allow a police car passage, the driver fired warning shots into the air and rammed into the crowd, hitting five people. Muslims set fire to the house of a Coptic Christian, who was accused of molesting a neighbor child, adding to sectarian tensions in Egypts south.

Thousands of residents of the restive Suez Canal city of Port Said in eastern Egypt rallied Monday in a funeral procession for civilians killed during street battles with security forces, as military police deployed to secure the gathering.

Parts of a government complex were set on fire as protesters throwing stones and firebombs clashed with police firing tear gas and birdshot Monday in a new day of clashes that have caught the military in the middle of fighting in the restive Egyptian city of Port Said.The renewed clashes came hours after thousands of city residents rallied in a funeral procession. Two more protesters died as a result of the Monday clashes between police and protesters. Al-Sayad said one of those who died was not involved in the clashes and was killed by live bullets fired by police. A Ministry of Interior official stated that one policeman died Monday as a result of injuries sustained during Sundays clashes.

Clashes between protesters and police have broken out in Port Said despite efforts by the military to separate the two sides. Protesters hurled stones at police, prompting the police to respond with tear gas. 157 injured.

In Port Said, a city that for weeks has been in open rebellion against the Islamist president, several hundred people, many of them relatives of the defendants, gathered outside the local government offices to vent their anger. They chanted slogans against Morsis government and the verdicts.

Several protesters in Port Said, a strategic city at the Mediterranean end of the Suez Canal, sought to disrupt shipping in the vital waterway by releasing small speedboats into the traffic lanes, although the effort failed to disrupt shipping. Others set fire to tires on the cities dock to prevent ships from coming in, but that again was quickly abandoned.

In unrelated violence, at least two protesters were killed on Saturday in clashes between riot police and stone-throwing demonstrators on a Nile-side street in central Cairo. One of the dead persons suffocated from inhaling teargas and the other died from a rubber bullet wound, the source confirmed. Nineteen others were injured in the clashes near two luxury hotels and the U.S. and British embassies.

Shortly after the verdict was announced Saturday, suspected fans of Cairos Al-Ahly club who had gathered in the thousands outside the teams headquarters in central Cairo went on a rampage, torching a police club nearby and storming Egypts soccer federation headquarters before setting it ablaze. The Egyptian Football Federation decided subsequently to halt all football activity in Egypt for an indefinite period, reported AlArabaiya News Channel.

Protesters burned a flag belonging to the Libyan Embassy in Cairo on Monday to protest the death in prison of an Egyptian Christian suspected of proselytizing in Libya. The protesters are also angry over reports that around 100 Coptic Egyptians are being held by an ultraconservative Islamist Libyan militia.

On Saturday, dozens of bakery owners who receive subsidized wheat from the government protested in Cairo against the austerity measures and shortages of diesel fuel. The protest was held amid economic reforms the government is seeking to boost the economy and ensure that subsidized bread reaches millions of poor Egyptians who complain that the bakeries sell the wheat for a profit

Police fired tear gas to disperse thousands of supporters and opponents of President Mohammed Morsi during clashes that erupted on Saturday as he launched development projects in southern Egyptian where residents have long complained of being neglected by the central government. The rioting came as Morsi was trying to reach out to residents of Sohag, one of Egypts poorest southern cities.

Dozens of journalists are protesting against what they claim were assaults on their colleagues by members of Egypts Muslim Brotherhood from which President Mohammed Morsi hails. They protested Sunday outside the Journalists Syndicate in the Egyptian capital, Cairo

Hundreds of Muslim villagers in Egypts south have attacked Christian-owned stores in search of a girl whose family claims was abducted. The villagers assaulted the stores Tuesday and surrounded two churches in the city of al-Wasta in Bani Suef province in Egypts south. Security forces guarded the churches. No casualties were reported.The college-aged girl disappeared around one month ago. The crowd accused local Christian of kidnapping her.
On 21 March 2013, Egyptian witnesses and officials say villagers in Ezbat el-Gindy beat to death a young man after catching him trying to steal a car in a new case of vigilante violence.

Egyptian protesters have ransacked an office of the Muslim Brotherhoods political party in the northern coastal city of Alexandria. It was the second such attack on an office of the Islamist group on Friday. The first was in the Cairo neighborhood of Manial. Both attacks happened as opponents and supporters of the Brotherhood clashed near the groups headquarters in Cairo.

It was the second such attack on an office of the Islamist group on Friday. The first was in the Cairo neighborhood of Manial. Both attacks happened as opponents and supporters of the Brotherhood clashed near the groups headquarters in Cairo. At least 190 people were injured as bloody clashes erupted Friday between Muslim Brotherhoods (MB) supporters and opponents near the groups headquarters in the Egyptian capital of Cairo and other governorates across Egypt,

An Egyptian security official and a priest say police fired tear gas and clashed with a stone-throwing mob of Muslims who had surrounded a Coptic Church in anger over an inter-faith romance. The alleged romance ignited sectarian tension in Wasta, a rural town in Beni Suef province, about 95 kilo-meters (60 miles) south of Cairo. Clashes flared anew on Friday after weekly Muslim prayers.For more than a month, Muslims have attacked churches over the incident and forced Christians to close their shops in the town.
Egyptian security forces have fired tear gas at protesters hurling firebombs at them in central Cairo, hours after hundreds of opponents of Egypts President Mohammed Morsi rallied peacefully
Hundreds of Coptic Christians visiting a southern monastery have protested after coming under attack by unidentified assailants who stole their money and personal belongings and beat them.

On 9 June 2013, a security official says villagers abducted a man in northern Egypt and beat him to death. The incident occurred in the governorate of Damietta. The villagers killed a carpenter they accused of killing a minibus driver.

Confrontations between campaigners of the 'Tamarod' (Rebel) movement and supporters of President Mohamed Mursi broke out. Muslim Brotherhood members were accused of throwing Molotov cocktails. Between 3 and 16 people injured.
Protesters continued their sit-in for a second day at the governors office, sealing off the gates
Protesters blocked a mosque where a Muslim Brotherhood cleric was scheduled to visit and clashed with Brotherhood members. A car exploded but no one was hurt.

Clashes in Tanta, between Muslim Brotherhood members and activists demonstrating against the appointment of a new governor drawn from the Brotherhood were met with teargas by Egyptian police. Protesters then tried to set fire to the offices of the Muslim Brotherhoods party in the city, hurling firebombs and smashing the buildings doors, as well as damaging vehicles believed to belong to Brotherhood members. 26 injured.

Tens of thousands of supporters of Egypts Islamist president rallied on Friday along a main boulevard near Cairos presidential palace in a show of force against opponents demanding his ouster amid increasing tension and polarization in Egypt
Hundreds of protesters in the nearby city of Bassioun hurled stones at Freedom and Justice Party offices, tearing down the party sign.
Thousands of supporters of Egypts embattled president are rallying in the nations capital in a show of support ahead of what are expected to be massive opposition-led protests on June 30.

Egyptian security officials say that one person was killed in street fighting between supporters and opponents of Egypts Islamist president. Security officials say the latest death came in clashes Friday in the Nile Delta city of Mansoura, where two other people were killed in previous days.

The health department says one person has died and at least 85 others were injured in clashes between supporters and opponents of Egypts President Mohamed Morsi in the Mediterranean city of Alexandria.

At least six people were injured when an anti-Morsi march was attacked by the presidents supporters in the city of Samanod, according to a security official. Attackers fired gunshots and threw acid at the protesters as they passed the house of a local Brotherhood leader.
In the Delta city of Tanta, four men believed to be Morsi supporters tried to attack a mosque preacher during his sermon.

Alexandria security chief Gen. Amin Ezz Eddin told Al-Jazeera TV that an American was killed Friday in Sidi Gabr Square while photographing the battles between opposition youth and members of the Muslim Brotherhood, from which Morsi hails.
The headquarters of Egypts Muslim Brotherhood was attacked on Sunday with petrol bombs, the Islamist movement and security officials said.
A large number of demonstrators have started heading to Mostafa Mahmoud Mosque in a separate demonstration joining the larger crowd in Tahrir Square.
Thousands of Islamists gathered in a show of support for Morsi outside the Rabia al-Adawiya Mosque near the Ittihadiya presidential palace, which the opposition planned to march on in the evening.

Hundreds of thousands of opponents of Egypts Islamist president massed in Cairos Tahrir Square and in cities around the country Sunday, launching an all-out push to force Mohammed Morsi from office on the one-year anniversary of his inauguration. 27 injured.

Enraged by the killing of an anti-Morsi protester by an unidentified Islamist militia, protesters marched to the office of the Freedom and Justice party, political arm of President Mohammed Morsis Muslim Brotherhood. Gunmen inside the building opened fire, killing at least two.

In a potentially volatile confrontation after nightfall, however, several dozen youths attacked the headquarters of Morsis Muslim Brotherhood on a plateau overlooking the capital. They threw stones and firebombs at the building, and people inside the walled villa fired at the attackers with birdshot.
a young man in his 20s, died in hospital after being seriously injured in an armed attack on protesters against controversial President Mohamed Morsi in Beni Suef.

Security officials say suspected Islamists have killed three protesters in the southern city of Assiut. The officials said Islamists on a motorbike opened fire on protesters outside the local government building in Assiut, killing one and wounding seven.
In southern Egypt, Islamists attacked the main church in the city of Qena.
In the town of Dabaiya near the city of Luxor, a mob torched houses of Christians, sending dozens seeking shelter in a police station. A Muslim man died.
Protests were held in the Mediterranean city of Alexandria
Protesters in Cairo took to the streets to also protest the deaths of three women killed by unidentified assailants at a Brotherhood rally in the Nile Delta city of Mansoura

Several hundreds people protesting against the government clashed with government supporters in Alexandria. Soldiers and plain clothes agents prevented a CBS News foreign correspondent and her crew from filming demonstrations and 'marched them at gunpoint' to their hotel.

Al-Jazeeras offices in Cairo were stormed and torched by unidentified individuals. Its website was also allegedly hacked. At the same time, unidentified individuals stormed Al-Hurras Cairo news bureau and threatened to kill two journalists on-air, leading to the immediate closure of the station.
Thousands protested in a number of cities, including Port Said.
Hundreds of state electricity workers protested outside the offices of the South Cairo Electricity company to demand the departure of its director.
Several hundred workers of a silk factory and fuel coke plant in Hlewan protested to demand better pay and working conditions.

More than 10,000 protesters tore apart barricades set up by the military outside the State TV and Radio building and protested outside it, blocking employees from entering. Soldiers were nearby but did not intervene.

40,000 protesters marched down a main avenue in Asyut to demand the departure of President Mubarak. They split up to protest in front of the main security HQ and provincial government HQ. Riot police were on hand but did not intervene.
A large number of people protested in Sohag and Minya, marching on local government HQs.
Tens of thousands marched to demand the departure of President Mubarak in Shebin el-Kom.

Police opened fire on protesters in the town of Deirout in Assiut governorate, injuring at least 4 after protesters threw rocks at a police station.
Dozens of graduates of archeology schools demonstrated outside the Ministry of Antiquities to demand jobs.

Several hundreds workers protested outside the state-run Egyptian Trade Unions Federation (ETUF) building demanding the dissolution of its board who they accuse of corruption. The workers traded bottles, stones and bricks with board supporters inside the building until soldiers separated the two sides.
About 1,000 people demonstrated to demand that a chemical factory be closed in Port Said for alleged waste dumping in a lake near the city.
Hundreds of Cairo airport employees protested inside the arrivals terminal at the Cairo International Airport to demand better wages and health benefits.

Workers from Egypts largest textile factory relaunched a strike over pay and to demand investigation into corruption at the factory in Mahallah al-Koubra. The strike had been suspended on Feb.13. Several hundred workers also held a sit-in in the factorys courtyard in front of the administrative building.

About 15,000 workers of the Misr Spinning and Weaving Company factory in Mahalla al-Kubra (one of Egypts largest factories) held sit-ins from Feb.16-19 in front of the companies administrative buildings in the city.

Hundreds of thousands of people rallied outside the main mosque in Alexandira in the Ash Shatibi area and called for the departure of the new Shafiq government, employing the same chants used against Mubaraks government.

A group of students gather at the Pyramids to rally for tourism, which has been lacking since the popular uprising.

After a clash between a Muslim and a Christian family in Sawl over a relationship between two of their children leading to deaths on both sides, a crowd of Muslims protested in front of a church in the area and later broke into it and torched it after police/military personnel had escorted Christians in the building to safety.
About 2,000 Christians blocked a main road in the eastern part of Cairo and threw stones at motorists. They were protesting the burning of a church last week.

Thousands of police officers gather outside the Interior Ministry Building to protest for higher pay. They later set part of the building on fire during their protest. 8 people suffered from smoke inhalation due to the blaze.
On Wednesday morning a protestor was shot dead on Qasr Al-Nil bridge by 'unidentified assailants,' according to state affiliated news agency Al-Ahram.

On 12 February 2011, in Kum Umbu, during a peaceful march following Mubarak's resignation, unidentified gunmen opened fire on the crowd, killing one student and injuring four others. A group of 'thugs' also attempted to release prisoners from the police station, while 100 others burned archives of Kum Umbu Court. Several buildings were damaged.

On 15 February 2011, in Mohandiseen, unidentified men armed with swords entered the headquarters of Al Watani newspaper and assaulted journalists who have been participating for the second day in a sit-in to demand better working conditions.

On 16 February 2011, while a number of workers were protesting against corruption in front of the Education Directorate in Sohag, a group of unidentified armed men in a car shot at them. No injuries or fatalities reported.
